greek_head: Ἐπορευόμην μὲν ἐξ Ἀκαδημείας εὐθὺ Λυκείου τὴν ἔξω τείχους ὑπ' αὐτὸ τὸ τεῖχος ἐπειδὴ δ' ἐγενόμην κατὰ τὴν
greek_tokens: eforeuw men ek akadhmeia euqus lukeion o ecw teixos upo autos o teixos epei de gignomai kata o pulis h o panopos krhnh entauqa suntugxanw ippoqalei te o ierwnumos kai kthsippos o paianizw kai allos meta outos neaniskos aqroos sunestw kai egw proseimi o ippoqalhs eidon eimi swkraths fhmi poi dh poreuw kai
english_head: I was making my way from the Academy straight to the Lyceum, by the road outside the town wall,—just under the wall; and when I reached the little gate that leads to the spring of Panops, I chanced there upon Hippothales, son of Hieronymus, and Ctesippus of Paeania, and some other youths with them, standing in a group together. Then Hippothales, as he saw me approaching, said: Socrates, whither away, and whence?

greek_head: πόθεν Ἐξ Ἀκαδημείας ἦν δ' ἐγώ πορεύομαι εὐθὺ Λυκείου
greek_tokens: pinw ek akadhmeia eimi de egw poreuw euqus lukeion deuro dh eimi de os euqus egw ou paraballw acios mentoi poi fhmi egw legw kai para tis o su deuro fhmi deiknumi egw en o katantikru o teixos peribolos su tis kai qura anoignumi diatribw de eimi de os autoqi egw te autos kai allos panu polus kai kalos
english_head: From the Academy, I replied, on my way straight to the Lyceum. Come over here, he said, straight to us. You will not put in here? But you may as well. Where do you mean? I asked; and what is your company? Here, he said, showing me there, just opposite the wall, a sort of enclosure and a door standing open. We pass our time there, he went on; not only we ourselves, but others besides,—a great many, and handsome.

greek_head: Ἔστιν δὲ δὴ τί τοῦτο καὶ τίς ἡ διατριβή Παλαίστρα ἔφη νεωστὶ ᾠκοδομημένη ἡ δὲ διατριβὴ τὰ
greek_tokens: eimi de dh tis outos kai tis o diatribh palaistra fhmi newsti oikodomew o de diatribh o polus en logos os hdews an su metadidwmi kalos ge eimi de egw poiew didaskw de tis autoqi sos etairos ge eimi de os kai epaineths mikkos ma dios eimi de egw ou faulos ge anhr alla ikanos sofisths boulomai oun epomai fhmi ina kai eidon o eimi autoqi autos
english_head: And what, pray, is this place, and what your pastime? A wrestling school, he said, of recent construction; and our pastime chiefly consists of discussions, in which we should be happy to let you have a share. That is very good of you, I said; and who does the teaching there? Your own comrade, he replied, and supporter, Miccus. Upon my word, I said, he is no slight person, but a qualified professor. Then will you please come in with us, he said, so as to see for yourself the company we have there?

greek_head: Πρῶτον ἡδέως ἀκούσαιμ' ἂν ἐπὶ τῷ καὶ εἴσειμι καὶ τίς ὁ καλός
greek_tokens: proteros hdews akouw an epi o kai eiseimi kai tis o kalos allos fhmi allos egw dokew eimi swkraths sos de dh tis eimi ippoqales outos egw eipon kai os erwtaw eruqriaw kai egw eipon eimi pais ierwnumos ippoqales outos men mhketi eipon eite eraw tis eite mh oida gar ostis ou monos eraw alla kai prosw hdh ei poreuw o erws eimi de egw o men allos faulos
english_head: I should be glad to hear first on what terms I am to enter, and which is the handsome one. Each of us, he replied, has a different fancy, Socrates. Well, and which is yours, Hippothales? Tell me that. At this question he blushed; so I said: Ah, Hippothales, son of Hieronymus, you need not trouble to tell me whether you are in love with somebody or not: for I know you are not only in love, but also far advanced already in your passion. In everything else I may be a poor useless creature,

greek_head: καὶ ἄχρηστος τοῦτο δέ μοί πως ἐκ θεοῦ δέδοται ταχὺ οἵῳ τ' εἶναι γνῶναι ἐρῶντά τε καὶ ἐρώμενον
greek_tokens: kai axrhstos outos de egw pws ek qeaomai didwmi taxus oios su eimi gignwskw eraw te kai eraomai kai os aekwn polus eti mallon eruqriaw o oun kthsippos asteios ge eimi de os ostis eruqriaw eimi ippoqales kai oknew eipon swkratew onoma ean de outos kai mikros xronos sundiatribw su parateinw upo sew akouw qama legw egw goun eimi swkraths ekkwfow o
english_head: but there is one gift that I have somehow from heaven,—to be able to recognize quickly a lover or a beloved. When he heard this, he blushed much more than ever. Then Ctesippus remarked: Quite charming, the way you blush, Hippothales, and shrink from telling Socrates the name; yet, if he spends but a little time with you, he will find you a regular torment, as he hears you repeat it again and again. He has deafened our ears, I can tell you, Socrates, by cramming them with Lysis:

greek_head: ὦτα καὶ ἐμπέπληκε Λύσιδος ἂν μὲν δὴ καὶ ὑποπίῃ εὐμαρία ἡμῖν ἐστιν καὶ ἐξ ὕπνου ἐγρομένοις Λύσιδος οἴεσθαι τοὔνομα
greek_tokens: ous kai empiplhmi lusidos an men dh kai upopinw eumareia egw eimi kai ek upnon egrw lusidos oiomai onoma akouw kai o men katalogadhn dihgeomai deinos eimi ou panu tis deinos eimi alla epeidan o poihma egw epixeirhsis katantlew kai suggramma kai o eimi outos deinos ostis kai aeidw eis o paidikon fwnew qaumasios os egw deomai akouw anexw nun de erwtaw upo sew eruqriaw
english_head: let him be a trifle in liquor, and as likely as not we start out of our sleep fancying we hear the name of Lysis. The descriptions he gives us in conversation, though dreadful enough, are not so very bad: it is when he sets about inundating us with his poems and prose compositions. More dreadful than all, he actually sings about his favorite in an extraordinary voice, which we have the trial of hearing. And now, at a question from you, he blushes! Lysis apparently, I said, is somebody quite young:

greek_head: Ἔστιν δέ ἦν δ' ἐγώ ὁ Λύσις νέος τις ὡς ἔοικε τεκμαίρομαι δέ ὅτι ἀκούσας τοὔνομα οὐκ ἔγνων
greek_tokens: eimi de eimi de egw o lusis neos tis ws eoika tekmairomai de ostis aekwn onoma ou gignwskw ou gar panu fhmi tis autos onoma legw alla eti patroqen eponomazw dia o sfodra o pathr gignwskw autos epei eu oida ostis polus dew o eidos agnoew o pais ikanos gar kai apo monos outos gignwskw legw eimi de egw ostis eimi dhmokraths fhmi o aicwnews o presbus us eien eimi de egw eimi ippoqales ws gennaios kai neanikos outos o erws pantaxh aneuriskw kai egw eimi epideiknumi o
english_head: this I infer from the fact that I did not recognize the name when I heard it. That is because they do not usually call him by his name, he replied; he still goes by his paternal title, as his father is so very well known. You must, I am sure, be anything but ignorant of the boy’s appearance: that alone would be enough to know him by. Let me hear, I said, whose son he is. The eldest son, he replied, of Democrates of Aexone. Ah well, I said, Hippothales, what an altogether noble and gallant love you have discovered there! Now please go on and give me a performance like those that you give your friends here,

greek_head: καὶ τοῖσδε ἐπιδείκνυσαι ἵνα εἰδῶ εἰ ἐπίστασαι ἃ χρὴ ἐραστὴν περὶ παιδικῶν πρὸς αὐτὸν ἢ πρὸς ἄλλους λέγειν
greek_tokens: kai ode epideiknumi ina oida ei efisthmi o xrh erasths peri paidikon pros autos h pros allos legw outos de tis fhmi staqmaomai eimi swkraths os ode legw poteros eimi de egw kai o era ecarnos ei e legw ode ou egw fhmi alla mh poiew eis o paidikon mhde suggrafw ou ugiainw fhmi o kthsippos alla lhrew te kai mainomai kai egw eipon eimi ippoqales ou tis o metron deomai
english_head: so that I may know whether you understand what a lover ought to say of his favorite to his face or to others. Do you attach any weight, Socrates, he asked, to anything you have heard this fellow say? Tell me, I said; do you deny being in love with the person he mentions? Not I, he replied; but I do deny that I make poems and compositions on my favorite. He is in a bad way, said Ctesippus; why, he raves like a madman! Then I remarked: Hippothales, I do not want to hear your verses,

greek_head: ἀκοῦσαι οὐδὲ μέλος εἴ τι πεποίηκας εἰς τὸν νεανίσκον ἀλλὰ τῆς διανοίας ἵνα εἰδῶ τίνα τρόπον προσφέρῃ πρὸς τὰ
greek_tokens: akew oude melos ei tis poiew eis o neaniskos alla o dianoia ina oida tis tropos prosferw pros o paidikon ode dhpou su fhmi eraomai akribhs gar efisthmi kai mimnhskw eiper ws legw upo egw aei akouw diaqrulew nh o qeos fhmi o kthsippos panu ge kai gar eimi katagelastos eimi swkraths o gar erasths eimi kai diaferontws o allos o new prosexw o pais idios
english_head: or any ode that you may have indited to the youth; I only ask for their purport, that I may know your manner of dealing with your favorite. I expect this fellow will tell you, he replied: he has an accurate knowledge and recollection of them, if there is any truth in what he says of my having dinned them so constantly in his ears. Quite so, on my soul, said Ctesippus; and a ridiculous story it is too, Socrates. To be a lover, and to be singularly intent on one’s boy, yet to have nothing particular to tell him that a mere boy could not say, is surely ridiculous:

greek_head: μὲν μηδὲν ἔχειν λέγειν ὃ οὐχὶ κἂν παῖς εἴποι πῶς οὐχὶ καταγέλαστον ἃ δὲ ἡ πόλις ὅλη ᾄδει περὶ Δημοκράτους καὶ
greek_tokens: men mhdeis exw legw o ou kan pas eipon pws ou katagelastos o de o polis oloc aeidw peri dhmokraths kai lusidos o pappas o pais kai pas peri o progonos ploutos te kai ippotrofia kai nikh puqoi kai isqmoi kai nemw teqrippos te kai kelhs outos poiew te kai legw pros de outos eti outos kronikos o gar o hraklehs cenismos prwios egw en poihma tis dieimi ws dia o o hraklehs suggeneia o progonos autos
english_head: but he only writes and relates things that the whole city sings of, recalling Democrates and the boy’s grandfather Lysis and all his ancestors, with their wealth and the horses they kept, and their victories at Delphi, the Isthmus, and Nemea, with chariot-teams and coursers, and, in addition, even hoarier antiquities than these. Only two days ago he was recounting to us in some poem of his the entertainment of Hercules,—how on account of his kinship with Hercules their forefather welcomed the hero,

greek_head: ὑποδέξαιτο τὸν Ἡρακλέα γεγονὼς αὐτὸς ἐκ Διός τε καὶ τῆς τοῦ δήμου ἀρχηγέτου θυγατρός ἅπερ αἱ γραῖαι ᾄδουσι καὶ
greek_tokens: upodexomai o hraklea gignomai autos ek zeus te kai o o dhmos arxhgeths qugathr o o graih aeidw kai allos polus toioutos eimi swkraths outos eimi o outos legos te kai aeidw anagkazw kai egw akroaomai kai egw aekwn eipon eimi katagelastos ippoqales prin nikaw poiew te kai aeidw eis sautou egkwmios alla ou eis emautou fhmi eimi swkraths oute poiew oute aeidw ou oiomai ge eimi de egw o de pws exis fhmi
english_head: being himself the offspring of Zeus and of the daughter of their deme’s founder; such old wives’ tales, and many more of the sort, Socrates,—these are the things he tells and trolls, while compelling us to be his audience. When I heard this I said: Oh, you ridiculous Hippothales, do you compose and chant a triumph song on yourself, before you have won your victory? It is not on myself, Socrates, he replied, that I either compose or chant it. You think not, I said. Then what is the truth of it? he asked.

greek_head: Πάντων μάλιστα εἶπον εἰς σὲ τείνουσιν αὗται αἱ ᾠδαί ἐὰν μὲν γὰρ ἕλῃς τὰ παιδικὰ τοιαῦτα ὄντα κόσμος σοι
greek_tokens: pas malista eipon eis sos teinw outos o aoidh ean men gar airew o paidikon toioutos eimi kosmos su eimi o legw kai aeidw kai o eimi egkwmios wsper nikaw ostis toioutos paidikon tugxanw ean de su diafeugw osos an megas su erw eimi egkwmios peri o paidikon tosoutos megas docis kalh te kai agaqos sterew
english_head: Most certainly, I replied, it is you to whom these songs refer. For if you prevail on your favorite, and he is such as you describe, all that you have spoken and sung will be so much glory to you, and a veritable eulogy upon your triumph in having secured such a favorite as that: whereas if he eludes your grasp, the higher the terms of your eulogy of your favorite, the greater will seem to be the charms and virtues you have lost, and you will be ridiculed accordingly. Hence anyone who deals wisely in love-matters,

greek_head: καταγέλαστος εἶναι ὅστις οὖν τὰ ἐρωτικά ὦ φίλε σοφός οὐκ ἐπαινεῖ τὸν ἐρώμενον πρὶν ἂν ἕλῃ δεδιὼς τὸ μέλλον ὅπῃ
greek_tokens: katagelastos eimi ostis oun o erwtikos eimi filos sofos ou epainew o eraomai prin an airew deidw o mellw oph apobainw kai ama e kalos epeidan tis autos epainew kai auch fronhma empiplhmi kai megalauxia h ou oiomai egw fhmi oukoun osos an megalauxos eimi dusalwtos gignomai eoika ge poios tis oun an su dokew qhreuths eimi ei anasobew qhreuw kai dusalwtos o agra poiew
english_head: my friend, does not praise his beloved until he prevails, for fear of what the future may have in store for him. And besides, these handsome boys, when so praised and extolled, become full of pride and haughtiness: do you not think so? I do, he said. And then, the haughtier they are, the harder grows the task of capturing them? Yes, apparently. And what do you think of a hunter who should scare away his quarry in hunting and make it harder to catch? Clearly he would be a poor one.

greek_head: Δῆλον ὅτι φαῦλος Καὶ μὲν δὴ λόγοις τε καὶ ᾠδαῖς μὴ κηλεῖν ἀλλ' ἐξαγριαίνειν
greek_tokens: dhlos ostis faulos kai men dh logos te kai aoidh mh khlew alla ecagriainw polus amousia eimi gar dokew egw skopaw dh eimi ippoqales opws mh pas outos enoxos sautou poihsis dia o poihsis kaitoi oimh egw anhr poihsis blaptw eautou ou an su eqelw omologew ws agaqos pote eimi poihths blaberos eimi eautou ou ma o dios fhmi polus gar an alogia eaw alla dia
english_head: And hence to use speech and song, not for charming but for driving wild, would be gross fatuity, would it not? I think so. Then take care, Hippothales, not to make yourself guilty of all these things by your verse-making; and yet I fancy you will not like to allow that a man who damages himself by poetry can be a good poet, so long as he is damaging to himself. On my soul, no, he said; of course it would be most absurd. But this is the very reason, Socrates, why I impart my feelings to you,

greek_head: ταῦτα δή σοι ὦ Σώκρατες ἀνακοινοῦμαι καὶ εἴ τι ἄλλο ἔχεις συμβούλευε τίνα ἄν τις λόγον διαλεγόμενος ἢ τί
greek_tokens: outos dh su eimi swkraths anakoinew kai ei tis allos exis sumbouleuw tis an tis logos dialegw h tis prassw prosfilhs paidikon gignomai ou radios eimi de egw eipon alla ei egw eqelw autos poiew eis logos erxomai isos an dunamai su epideiknumi o xrh autos dialegw anti outos os outos legw te kai aeidw fhmi su alla oudeis fhmi xalepos an gar eiserxomai meta kthsippos ode kai kataezomai dialegw oimh men kai autos su proseimi filhkoos gar eimi swkraths diaferontws
english_head: and ask you for any useful advice you can give as to what conversation or conduct will help to endear one to one’s favorite. That is not an easy thing to tell, I replied; but if you will agree to get him to have a talk with me, I daresay I could show you an example of the conversation you should hold with him, instead of those things that your friends say you speak and sing. There is no difficulty about that, he said. If you will go in with Ctesippus here, and take a seat and talk, I think he will come to you of his own accord; he is singularly fond of listening, Socrates,

greek_head: ἐστίν καὶ ἅμα ὡς Ἑρμαῖα ἄγουσιν ἀναμεμειγμένοι ἐν ταὐτῷ εἰσιν οἵ τε νεανίσκοι καὶ οἱ παῖδες πρόσεισιν οὖν σοι
greek_tokens: eimi kai ama ws ermaios agw anamignumi en tautazw eimi e te neaniskos kai e pais proseimi oun su ei de mh kthsippos sunhqhs eimi dia o outos aneyios menecenos menecenos men gar dh pas malista etairos eimi tugxanw kalew oun outos autos ean ara mh proseimi autos outos eimi de egw xrh poiew kai ama lambanw o
english_head: and besides, they are keeping the Hermaea, so that the youths and boys are all mingled together. So he will come to you but if he does not, Ctesippus is intimate with him, as being a cousin of Menexenus; for Lysis has chosen Menexenus for his particular friend. So let Ctesippus call him if you find that he does not come of himself. That is what I must do, I said. Whereupon I took Ctesippus

greek_head: Κτήσιππον προσῇα εἰς τὴν παλαίστραν οἱ δ' ἄλλοι ὕστεροι ἡμῶν ᾖσαν
greek_tokens: kthsippos proseimi eis o palaistra e de allos usteros egw aeidw eiserxomai de katalambanw autoqi quw te o pais kai o peri o iereion sxedon tis hdh poiew astragalizw te dh kai kosmew apas e men oun polus en o aulew paizw ecw e de tis o apoduthrion en gwnia artiazw astragalos pampolus ek formiskos tis proairew outos de periisthmi allos qewrew os dh kai o lusis eimi kai isthmi en
english_head: with me into the wrestling school, and the others came after us. When we got inside, we found that the boys had performed the sacrifice in the place and, as the ceremonial business was now almost over, they were all playing at knuckle-bones and wearing their finest attire. Most of them were playing in the court out-of-doors; but some were at a game of odd-and-even in a corner of the undressing room, with a great lot of knuckle-bones which they drew from little baskets; and there were others standing about them and looking on. Among these was Lysis: he stood among the boys

greek_head: τοῖς παισί τε καὶ νεανίσκοις ἐστεφανωμένος καὶ τὴν ὄψιν διαφέρων οὐ τὸ καλὸς εἶναι μόνον ἄξιος ἀκοῦσαι ἀλλ' ὅτι
greek_tokens: o pais te kai neaniskos stefanow kai o oyis diaferw ou o kalos eimi monos acios akew alla ostis kalos te agaqos kai egw eis o katantikru apoxwrew katakaqezomai eimi gar autoqi hsuxia kai tis allhlwn dialegw peristrefw oun o lusis qama episkopew egw kai dhlos eimi epiqumew proserxomai teos men oun aporew te kai oknew monos proseimi epeita o menecenos
english_head: and youths with a garland on his head, a distinguished figure, deserving not merely the name of well-favored, but also of well-made and well-bred. As for us, we went and sat apart on the opposite side—for it was quiet there—and started some talk amongst ourselves. The result was that Lysis ever and anon turned round to observe us, and was obviously eager to join us. For a while, however, he hesitated, being too shy to approach us alone;

greek_head: ἐκ τῆς αὐλῆς μεταξὺ παίζων εἰσέρχεται καὶ ὡς εἶδεν ἐμέ τε καὶ τὸν Κτήσιππον ᾔει παρακαθιζησόμενος ἰδὼν οὖν αὐτὸν
greek_tokens: ek o aulew metacu paizw eiserxomai kai ws eidon egw te kai o kthsippos eimi parakaqizanw eidon oun autos o lusis epomai kai parakaqezomai meta o menecenos proserxomai dh kai e allos kai dh kai o ippoqalhs epei plews oraw efisthmi outos ephlugazw prosisthmi h mh oiomai kaqoraw o lusis deidw mh autos apexqanomai kai outws prosisthmi akroaomai kai egw pros o menecenos apoblepw eimi pais dhmofwn
english_head: till Menexenus stepped in for a moment from his game in the court and, on seeing me and Ctesippus, came to take a seat beside us. When Lysis saw him, he came along too and sat down with Menexenus. Then all the others came to us also; and I must add that Hippothales, when he saw a good many of them standing there, stood so as to be screened by them, in a position where he thought Lysis would not catch sight of him, as he feared that he might irritate him; in this way he stood by and listened. Then I, looking at Menexenus, asked him: Son of Demophon, which is the elder of you two? It is a point in dispute between us, he replied.

greek_head: ἦν δ' ἐγώ πότερος ὑμῶν πρεσβύτερος Ἀμφισβητοῦμεν ἔφη
greek_tokens: eimi de egw poteros su presbus amfisbhtew fhmi oukoun kai opoteros gennaios erizw an eimi de egw panu ge fhmi kai meis opoteros ge kallos wsautws gelaw oun amfw ou meis opoteros ge fhmi plousios su ou eromai filos gar eimi eimi gar panu ge fhmi oukoun koinos o ge filos legw wste outos ge oudeis diaferw eiper alhqhs peri o filios legw sumfhmi
english_head: Then you must also be at variance, I said, as to which is the nobler. Yes, to be sure, he said. And moreover, which is the more beautiful, likewise. This made them both laugh. But of course I shall not ask, I said, which of you is the wealthier; for you are friends, are you not? Certainly we are, they replied. And, you know, friends are said to have everything in common, so that here at least there will be no difference between you, if what you say of your friendship is true. They agreed.

greek_head: Ἐπεχείρουν δὴ μετὰ τοῦτο ἐρωτᾶν ὁπότερος δικαιότερος καὶ σοφώτερος αὐτῶν εἴη μεταξὺ οὖν τις προσελθὼν ἀνέστησε
greek_tokens: epixeirew dh meta outos erwtaw opoteros dikaios kai sofos autos eaw metacu oun tis proserxomai anisthmi o menecenos faskw kalew o paidotribhs dokew gar egw ieropoiew tugxanw ekeinos men oun oixomai egw de o lusis arow eimi pou eimi de egw eimi lusis sfodra filew su o pathr kai o mhthr panu ge eimi de os oukoun boulomai an su ws eudaimwn eimi
english_head: After that I was proceeding to ask them which was the juster and wiser of the two, when I was interrupted by somebody who came and fetched away Menexenus, saying that the wrestling-master was calling him: I understood that he was taking some part in the rites. So he went off; and then I asked Lysis: I suppose, Lysis, your father and mother are exceedingly fond of you? Yes, to be sure, he replied. Then they would like you to be as happy as possible?

greek_head: Πῶς γὰρ οὔ Δοκεῖ δέ σοι εὐδαίμων εἶναι ἄνθρωπος δουλεύων τε καὶ ᾧ μηδὲν ἐξείη ποιεῖν ὧν ἐπιθυμοῖ Μὰ
greek_tokens: pws gar ou dokew de su eudaimwn eimi anqrwpos douleuw te kai os mhdeis ecesti poiew os epiqumew ma diw ou egw fhmi oukoun ei su filew o pathr kai o mhthr kai eudaimwn su epiqumew gignomai outos pas tropos dhlos ostis proqumeomai opws an eudaimonew pws gar ou fhmi eaw ara su o boulomai poiew kai oudeis epiplhssw oude diakwluw poiew os an epiqumew nai ma dios egw ge eimi swkraths kai mala ge polus kwluw pws legw eimi de egw boulomai su makarios
english_head: Yes, of course. Do you consider that a man is happy when enslaved and restricted from doing everything he desires? Not I, on my word, he said. Then if your father and mother are fond of you, and desire to see you happy, it is perfectly plain that they are anxious to secure your happiness. They must be, of course, he said. Hence they allow you to do what you like, and never scold you, or hinder you from doing what you desire? Yes, they do, Socrates, I assure you: they stop me from doing a great many things. How do you mean? I said: they wish you

greek_head: εἶναι διακωλύουσι τοῦτο ποιεῖν ὃ ἂν βούλῃ ὧδε δέ μοι λέγε ἢν ἐπιθυμήσῃς ἐπί τινος τῶν τοῦ πατρὸς ἁρμάτων
greek_tokens: eimi diakwluw outos poiew o an boulomai wde de egw legos ean epiqumew epi tis o o pathr arma oxaomai lambanw o hnia otan amillaomai ou an eaw su alla diakwluw ma diw ou mentoi an fhmi eaw alla tis meis eimi tis hnioxos para o pathr misqos ferw pws legw misqwtos mallon epitrepw h sos poiew ostis an boulomai peri o ippos kai proseti
english_head: to be happy, and yet hinder you from doing what you like? But answer me this: suppose you desire to ride in one of your father’s chariots and hold the reins in some race; they will not allow you, but will prevent you? That is so, to be sure, he said; they will not allow me. But whom would they allow? There is a driver, in my father’s pay. What do you say? A hireling, whom they trust rather than you, so that he can do whatever he pleases with the horses; and they pay him besides a salary for doing that!

greek_head: αὐτοῦ τούτου ἀργύριον τελοῦσιν Ἀλλὰ τί μήν ἔφη Ἀλλὰ τοῦ ὀρικοῦ ζεύγους οἶμαι ἐπιτρέπουσίν σοι ἄρχειν
