english_head: Why, of course, he said. Well, but they trust you with the control of the mule-cart, and if you wanted to take the whip and lash the team, they would let you? Nothing of the sort, he said. Why, I asked, is nobody allowed to lash them? Oh yes, he said, the muleteer. Is he a slave, or free? A slave, he replied. So it seems that they value a slave more highly than you, their son, and entrust him rather than you with their property,

greek_head: διακωλύουσι καί μοι ἔτι τόδε εἰπέ σὲ αὐτὸν ἐῶσιν ἄρχειν σεαυτοῦ ἢ οὐδὲ τοῦτο ἐπιτρέπουσί σοι Πῶς γάρ ἔφη
greek_tokens: diakwluw kai egw eti ode eipon sos autos eaw arxw sautou h oude outos epitrepw su pws gar fhmi epitrepw alla arxw tis su ode paidagwgos fhmi mhs doulos eimi alla tis meis hmeteros ge fhmi eimi deinos eimi de egw eleuqeros eimi upo doulos arxw tis de poa au outos o paidagwgos su arxw agw dhpou fhmi eis didaskalos mhs mh kai outos su arxw e
english_head: and allow him to do what he likes, while preventing you? And now there is one thing more you must tell me. Do they let you control your own self, or will they not trust you in that either? Of course they do not, he replied. But some one controls you? Yes, he said, my tutor here. Is he a slave? Why, certainly; he belongs to us, he said. What a strange thing, I exclaimed; a free man controlled by a slave! But how does this tutor actually exert his control over you? By taking me to school, I suppose, he replied. And your schoolmasters, can it be that they also control you?

greek_head: διδάσκαλοι Πάντως δήπου Παμπόλλους ἄρα σοι δεσπότας καὶ ἄρχοντας ἑκὼν ὁ πατὴρ ἐφίστησιν ἀλλ' ἆρα
greek_tokens: didaskalos pantws dhpou pampolus ara su despoths kai arxw ekwn o pathr efisthmi alla ara epeidan oikade erxomai para o mhthr ekeinos su eaw poiew ostis an boulomai ina autos makarios eimi h peri o erion h peri o istos otan ufainw ou tis gar pou diakwluw su h o spaqh h o kerkis h allos tis o peri talasiourgia organon aptw kai os gelaw ma dios fhmi eimi
english_head: I should think they do! Then quite a large number of masters and controllers are deliberately set over you by your father. But when you come home to your mother, she surely lets you do what you like, that she may make you happy, either with her wool or her loom, when she is weaving? I take it she does not prevent you from handling her batten, or her comb, or any other of her wool-work implements. At this he laughed and said: I promise you, Socrates, not only does she prevent me, but I should get a beating as well,

greek_head: Σώκρατες οὐ μόνον γε διακωλύει ἀλλὰ καὶ τυπτοίμην ἂν εἰ ἁπτοίμην Ἡράκλεις ἦν δ' ἐγώ μῶν μή τι ἠδίκηκας τὸν
greek_tokens: swkraths ou monos ge diakwluw alla kai tuptw an ei aptw hraklehs eimi de egw mhs mh tis adikew o pathr h o mhthr ma diw ou egw fhmi alla anti tis meis outws su deinos diakwluw eudaimwn eimi kai poiew ostis an boulomai kai dia hmeros oloc trefw su aei tis douleuw kai eis logos oligos os epiqumew oudeis poiew wste su ws eoika oute o xrhma tosoutos eimi oudeis ofelos alla pas
english_head: if I laid hands on them. Good heavens! I said; can it be that you have done your father or mother some wrong? On my word, no, he replied. Well, what reason can they have for so strangely preventing you from being happy and doing what you like? Why do they maintain you all day long in constant servitude to somebody, so that, in a word, you do hardly a single thing that you desire? And thus, it would seem, you get no advantage from all your great possessions—

greek_head: αὐτῶν μᾶλλον ἄρχουσιν ἢ σύ οὔτε τοῦ σώματος οὕτω γενναίου ὄντος ἀλλὰ καὶ τοῦτο ἄλλος ποιμαίνει καὶ θεραπεύει
greek_tokens: autos mallon arxw h su oute o swma outws gennaios eimi alla kai outos allos poimainw kai qerapeuw su de arxw oudeis eimi lusis oude poiew oudeis os epiqumew ou gar pw fhmi hlikia exw eimi swkraths mh ou outos su eimi pais dhmokraths kwluw epei o ge tososde ws oimh kai o pathr kai o mhthr su epitrepw kai ou anamenw ews an hlikia exw otan gar boulomai eautou tis anagignwskw h grafw sos ws oimh
english_head: nay, anyone else controls them rather than you—nor from your own person, though so well-born, which is also shepherded and managed by another; while you, Lysis, control nobody, and do nothing that you desire. It is because I am not yet of age, Socrates, he said. That can hardly be the hindrance, son of Democrates, since there is a certain amount, I imagine, that your father and mother entrust to you without waiting until you come of age. For when they want some reading or writing done for them, it is you,

greek_head: πρῶτον τῶν ἐν τῇ οἰκίᾳ ἐπὶ τοῦτο τάττουσιν ἦ γάρ Πάνυ γ' ἔφη Οὐκοῦν ἔξεστί σοι ἐνταῦθ' ὅτι ἂν βούλῃ
greek_tokens: proteros o en o oikia epi outos tassw eimi gar panu ge fhmi oukoun ecesti su entauqa ostis an boulomai proteros o gramma grafw kai ostis an deuteros kai anagignwskw wsautws ecesti kai epeidan ws oimh o lura lambanw ou diakwluw su oute o pathr oute o mhthr epiteinw te kai anihmi os an boulomai o xordh kai yallw kai krouw o plhktron h diakwluw ou dhta tis pote an oun eaw eimi lusis o aitios ostis entauqa
english_head: I conceive, whom they appoint to do it before any others of the household. Is it not so? Quite so, he replied. And you are free there to choose which letter you shall write first and which second, and you have a like choice in reading. And, I suppose, when you take your lyre, neither your father nor your mother prevents you from tightening or slackening what string you please, or from using your finger or your plectrum at will: or do they prevent you? Oh, no. Then whatever can be the reason, Lysis, why they do not prevent you here,

greek_head: μὲν οὐ διακωλύουσιν ἐν οἷς δὲ ἄρτι ἐλέγομεν κωλύουσι Ὅτι οἶμαι ἔφη ταῦτα μὲν ἐπίσταμαι ἐκεῖνα δ' οὔ Εἶεν
greek_tokens: men ou diakwluw en os de arti legw kwluw ostis oimh fhmi outos men efisthmi ekeinos de ou eien eimi de egw eimi aristos ou ara o hlikia su perimenw o pathr epitrepw pas alla h an hmeros hgeomai su beltiwn eautou fronew outos epitrepw su kai eautou kai o eautou oimh egw fhmi eien eimi de egw tis de o geitwn ara ou o autos oros os o pathr peri sew
english_head: while in the matters we were just mentioning they do? I suppose, he said, because I understand these things, but not those others. Very well, I said, my excellent friend: so it is not your coming of age that your father is waiting for, as the time for entrusting you with everything; but on the day when he considers you to have a better intelligence than himself, he will entrust you with himself and all that is his. Yes, I think so, he said. Very well, I went on, but tell me, does not your neighbor observe the same rule as your father towards you? Do you think he will entrust you with the management of his house, as soon as he considers you to have a better idea

greek_head: πότερον οἴει αὐτὸν ἐπιτρέψειν σοι τὴν αὑτοῦ οἰκίαν οἰκονομεῖν ὅταν σε ἡγήσηται βέλτιον περὶ οἰκονομίας ἑαυτοῦ
greek_tokens: poteros oiomai autos epitrepw su o eautou oikia oikonomew otan su hgeomai beltiwn peri oikonomia eautou fronew h autos epistatew egw epitrepw oimh tis de aqhnaios oiomai su ou epitrepw o eautou otan aisqanomai ostis ikanos fronew egw pros zeus eimi de egw tis ara o megas basileus poteros o presbus uios e o o asios arxh gignomai mallon an epitrepw epomai kreas emballw ostis an boulomai emballw
english_head: of its management than himself, or will he direct it himself? I should say he would entrust it to me. Well then, do you not think that the Athenians will entrust you with their affairs, when they perceive that you have sufficient intelligence? I do. Ah, do let me ask this, I went on: what, pray, of the Great King? Would he allow his eldest son, heir-apparent to the throne of Asia, to put what he chose into the royal stew,

greek_head: εἰς τὸν ζωμόν ἢ ἡμῖν εἰ ἀφικόμενοι παρ' ἐκεῖνον ἐνδειξαίμεθα αὐτῷ ὅτι ἡμεῖς κάλλιον φρονοῦμεν ἢ ὁ ὑὸς
greek_tokens: eis o zwmos h egw ei afikneomai para ekeinos endeiknumi autos ostis egw kallion fronew h o us autos peri oyon skeuasia egw dhlos ostis fhmi kai o men ge oude an mikros eaw emballw egw de kan ei boulomai drassomai o als eaw an emballw pws gar ou tis de ei o ofqalmos o us autos asqenew ara eaw an autos aptw o eautou
english_head: or would he prefer us to do it, supposing we came before him and convinced him that we had a better notion than his son of preparing a tasty dish? Clearly he would prefer us, he said. And he would not allow the prince to put in the smallest bit, whereas he would let us have our way even if we wanted to put in salt by the handful. Why, of course. Again, if his son has something the matter with his eyes, would he let him meddle with them himself, if he considered him to be no doctor,

greek_head: ὀφθαλμῶν μὴ ἰατρὸν ἡγούμενος ἢ κωλύοι ἄν Κωλύοι ἄν Ἡμᾶς δέ γε εἰ ὑπολαμβάνοι ἰατρικοὺς εἶναι κἂν
greek_tokens: ofqalmos mh iatros hgeomai h kwluw an kwluw an egw de ge ei upolambanw iatrikos eimi kan ei boulomai dianoigw o ofqalmos empassw o tefra oimh ou an kwluw hgeomai orqos fronew alhqhs legw ara oun kai allos pas egw epitrepw an mallon h eautou kai o uios peri osos an dokew autos sofos ekeinos eimi anagkh fhmi eimi swkraths outws ara exis eimi de egw eimi filos lusis eis men outos
english_head: or would he prevent him? He would prevent him. But if he supposed us to have medical skill, he would not prevent us, I imagine, even though we wanted to pull the eyes open and sprinkle them with ashes, so long as he believed our judgement to be sound. That is true. So he would entrust us, rather than himself or his son, with all his other affairs besides, wherever he felt we were more skilled than they? Necessarily, he said, Socrates. The case then, my dear Lysis, I said, stands thus: with regard to matters

greek_head: ἃ ἂν φρόνιμοι γενώμεθα ἅπαντες ἡμῖν ἐπιτρέψουσιν Ἕλληνές τε καὶ βάρβαροι καὶ ἄνδρες καὶ γυναῖκες ποιήσομέν
greek_tokens: o an fronimos gignomai apas egw epitrepw ellhn te kai barbaros kai anhr kai gunh poiew te en outos ostis an boulomai kai oudeis egw ekwn eimi empodizw alla autos te eleuqeros eimi en autos kai allos arxw hmeteros te outos eimi oneomai gar apo autos eis o de an new mh ktaomai oute tis egw epitrepw peri autos poiew o egw dokew alla empodizw
english_head: in which we become intelligent, every one will entrust us with them, whether Greeks or foreigners, men or women and in such matters we shall do as we please, and nobody will care to obstruct us. Nay, not only shall we ourselves be free and have control of others in these affairs, but they will also belong to us, since we shall derive advantage from them; whereas in all those for which we have failed to acquire intelligence, so far will anyone be from permitting us to deal with them as we think fit, that everybody will do his utmost to obstruct us—

greek_head: πάντες καθ' ὅτι ἂν δύνωνται οὐ μόνον οἱ ἀλλότριοι ἀλλὰ καὶ ὁ πατὴρ καὶ ἡ μήτηρ καὶ εἴ τι τούτων οἰκειότερόν
greek_tokens: pas kaqa ostis an dunamai ou monos e allotrios alla kai o pathr kai o mhthr kai ei tis outos oikeios eimi autos te en autos eimi allos uphkoos kai egw eimi allotrios oudeis gar apo autos oneomai sugxwrew outws exw sugxwrew ara oun tis filos eimi kai tis egw filhsis en outos en os an eimi anwfelhs ou dhta fhmi nun ara oude sos o pathr oude allos allos oudeis filew kaqa osos an eimi axrhstos ou eoika
english_head: not merely strangers, but father and mother and any more intimate person than they; and we on our part shall be subject to others in such matters, which will be no concern of ours, since we shall draw no advantage from them. Do you agree to this account of the case? I agree. Then will anyone count us his friends or have any affection for us in those matters for which we are useless? Surely not, he said. So now, you see, your father does not love you, nor does anyone love anyone else, so far as one is useless. Apparently not, he said. Then if you can become wise, my boy,

greek_head: ἔφη Ἐὰν μὲν ἄρα σοφὸς γένῃ ὦ παῖ πάντες σοι φίλοι καὶ πάντες σοι οἰκεῖοι ἔσονται χρήσιμος γὰρ καὶ ἀγαθὸς
greek_tokens: fhmi ean men ara sofos gignomai eimi pais pas su filos kai pas su oikeios eimi xrhsimos gar kai agaqos eimi ei de mh sos oute allos oudeis oute o pathr filos eimi oute o mhthr oute e oikeios oios te oun epi outos eimi lusis megas fronew en os tis mhpw fronew kai pws an fhmi ei de ara su didaskalos deomai oupw fronew alhqhs oude ara megalofrwn ei eiper afrwn eti ma dios fhmi eimi swkraths ou egw dokew
english_head: everybody will be your friend, every one will be intimate with you, since you will be useful and good; otherwise, no one at all, not your father, nor your mother, nor your intimate connections, will be your friends. Now is it possible, Lysis, to have a high notion of yourself in matters of which you have as yet no notion? Why, how can I? he said. Then if you are in need of a teacher, you have as yet no notion of things? True. Nor can you have a great notion of yourself, if you are still notionless. Upon my word, Socrates, he said, I do not see how I can.

greek_head: Καὶ ἐγὼ ἀκούσας αὐτοῦ ἀπέβλεψα πρὸς τὸν Ἱπποθάλη καὶ ὀλίγου ἐξήμαρτον ἐπῆλθε γάρ μοι εἰπεῖν ὅτι Οὕτω
greek_tokens: kai egw aekwn autos apoblepw pros o ippoqalh kai oligos ecamartanw eperxomai gar egw eipon ostis outws xrh eimi ippoqales o paidikon dialegw tapeinow kai sustellw alla mh wsper su xaunow kai diaqruptw kateidon oun autos agwniaw kai qorubew upo o legw anamimnhskw ostis kai prosisthmi lanqanw o lusis boulomai analambanw oun emautou kai
english_head: On hearing him answer this, I glanced at Hippothales, and nearly made a blunder, for it came into my mind to say: This is the way, Hippothales, in which you should talk to your favorite, humbling and reducing him, instead of puffing him up and spoiling him, as you do now. Well, I noticed that he was in an agony of embarrassment at what we had been saying, and I remembered how, in standing near, he wished to hide himself from Lysis.

greek_head: ἐπέσχον τοῦ λόγου καὶ ἐν τούτῳ ὁ Μενέξενος πάλιν ἧκεν καὶ ἐκαθέζετο παρὰ τὸν Λύσιν ὅθεν καὶ ἐξανέστη ὁ οὖν
greek_tokens: epwxato o logos kai en outos o menecenos palin hkw kai katakaqezomai para o lusis oqen kai ecanisthmi o oun lusis mala paidikos kai filikos laqrh o menecenos mikros pros egw legos fhmi eimi swkraths o kai egw legw eipon kai menecenos kai egw eipon outos men su autos erew eimi lusis pantws gar prosexw o new panu men oun fhmi peiraw toinun eimi de egw apomnhmoneuw autos ostis
english_head: So I checked myself and withheld this remark. In the meantime, Menexenus came back, and sat down by Lysis in the place he had left on going out. Then Lysis, in a most playful, affectionate manner, unobserved by Menexenus, said softly to me: Socrates, tell Menexenus what you have been saying to me. To which I replied: You shall tell it him yourself, Lysis; for you gave it your closest attention. I did, indeed, he said. Then try, I went on, to recollect it as well as you can,

greek_head: μάλιστα ἵνα τούτῳ σαφῶς πάντα εἴπῃς ἐὰν δέ τι αὐτῶν ἐπιλάθῃ αὖθίς με ἀνερέσθαι ὅταν ἐντύχῃς πρῶτον
greek_tokens: malista ina outos safhs pas eipon ean de tis autos epilanqanomai auqis egw aneromai otan entugxanw proteros alla poiew fhmi outos eimi swkraths panu sfodra eu eimi alla tis allos autos legos ina kai egw akouw ews an oikade oraw eimi apeimi alla xrh poiew outos eimi de egw epei ge kai su keleuw alla oraw opws epikourhsis egw ean egw elegxw epixeirew o menecenos h ou oida ostis eristikos eimi nai ma dios fhmi sfodra ge dia outos su kai boulomai
english_head: so that you tell him the whole of it clearly: but if you forget any of it, mind that you ask me for it again when next you meet me. I will do so, Socrates, he said, by all means, I assure you. But tell him something else, that I may hear it too, until it is time to go home. Well, I must do so, I said, since it is you who bid me. But be ready to come to my support, in case Menexenus attempts to refute me. You know what a keen disputant he is. Yes, on my word, very keen; that is why I want you to have a talk with him.

greek_head: σε αὐτῷ διαλέγεσθαι Ἵνα ἦν δ' ἐγώ καταγέλαστος γένωμαι
greek_tokens: su autos dialegw ina eimi de egw katagelastos gignomai ou ma dios fhmi alla ina autos kolazw pinw eimi de egw ou radios deinos gar o anqrwpos kthsippos maqhths pareimi de su autos ou oraw kthsippos mhdeis su fhmi melw eimi swkraths alla eimi dialegw autos dialekteon eimi de egw outos oun egw legw pros egw autos tis su fhmi o kthsippos autos monos estiaw egw de ou
english_head: So that I may make myself ridiculous? I said. No, no, indeed, he replied; I want you to trounce him. How can I? I asked. It is not easy, when the fellow is so formidable—a pupil of Ctesippus. And here—do you not see?—is Ctesippus himself. Take no heed of anyone, Socrates, he said; just go on and have a talk with him. I must comply, I said. Now, as these words passed between us,— What is this feast, said Ctesippus, that you two are having by yourselves, without allowing us a share in your talk?

greek_head: μεταδίδοτον τῶν λόγων Ἀλλὰ μήν ἦν δ' ἐγώ μεταδοτέον ὅδε γάρ τι ὧν λέγω
greek_tokens: metadidwmi o logos alla meis eimi de egw metadoteon ode gar tis os legos ou manqanw alla fhmi oiomai menecenos oida kai keleuw outos erwtaw tis oun eimi de os ou erwtaw alla eromai eimi de egw kai egw eipon eimi menecenos o an su eromai tugxanw gar ek pais epiqumew kthma tis wsper allos allos o men gar tis ippos
english_head: Well, well, I replied, we must give you a share. My friend here fails to understand something that I have been saying, but tells me he thinks Menexenus knows, and he urges me to question him. Why not ask him then? said he. But I am going to, I replied. Now please answer, Menexenus, whatever question I may ask you. There is a certain possession that I have desired from my childhood, as every one does in his own way. One person wants to get possession of horses,

greek_head: ἐπιθυμεῖ κτᾶσθαι ὁ δὲ κύνας ὁ δὲ χρυσίον ὁ δὲ τιμάς ἐγὼ δὲ πρὸς μὲν ταῦτα πρᾴως ἔχω πρὸς δὲ τὴν τῶν φίλων
greek_tokens: epiqumew ktaomai o de kuwn o de xrusion o de timh egw de pros men outos praos exw pros de o o filos ktaomai panu erwtikos kai boulomai an egw filos agaqos gignomai mallon h o ariston en anqrwpos ortuc h alektruwn kai nai ma dios egw mallon h ippos te kai kuwn oimh de nh o kuwn mallon h o dareios xrusion ktaomai dexomai polus proteros etairos mallon de h autos dareios outws egw filetairos tis eimi su
english_head: another dogs, another money, and another distinctions: of these things I reck little, but for the possession of friends I have quite a passionate longing, and would rather obtain a good friend than the best quail or cock in the world; yes, and rather, I swear, than any horse or dog. I believe, indeed, by the Dog, that rather than all Darius’s gold I would choose to gain a dear comrade—far sooner than I would Darius himself, so fond I am of my comrades.

greek_head: οὖν ὁρῶν σέ τε καὶ Λύσιν ἐκπέπληγμαι καὶ εὐδαιμονίζω ὅτι οὕτω νέοι ὄντες οἷοι τ' ἐστὸν τοῦτο τὸ κτῆμα ταχὺ καὶ
greek_tokens: oun oraw sos te kai lusis ekplhssw kai eudaimonizw ostis outws neos eimi oios su eimi outos o kthma taxus kai radios ktaomai kai su te outos outws filos ktaomai taxus te kai sfodra kai au outos sos egw de outws prosw eimi o kthma wste oude ostis tropos gignomai filos eteros eteros oida alla outos dh autos su boulomai eromai ate empeiros kai egw eipon epeidan tis tis filew poteros poteros
english_head: Accordingly, when I see you and Lysis together, I am quite beside myself, and congratulate you on being able, at such an early age, to gain this possession so quickly and easily; since you, Menexenus, have so quickly and surely acquired his friendship, and he likewise yours: whereas I am so far from acquiring such a thing, that I do not even know in what way one person becomes a friend of another, and am constrained to ask you about this very point, in view of your experience. Now tell me: when one person loves another, which of the two becomes friend of the other—

greek_head: φίλος γίγνεται ὁ φιλῶν τοῦ φιλουμένου ἢ ὁ φιλούμενος τοῦ φιλοῦντος ἢ οὐδὲν διαφέρει Οὐδέν ἔφη ἔμοιγε
greek_tokens: filos gignomai o filos o filew h o filew o filew h oudeis diaferw oudeis fhmi egw dokew diaferw pws legw eimi de egw amfoteros ara allhlwn filos gignomai ean monos o eteros o eteros filew egw fhmi dokew tis de ou eimi filew mh antifilew upo outos os an filew eimi tis de ara eimi kai misew filew oios pou eniote dokew kai e erasths pasxw pros o paidikon filew gar
english_head: the loving of the loved, or the loved of the loving? Or is there no difference? There is none, he replied, in my opinion. How is that? I said; do you mean that both become friends mutually, when there is only one loving the other? Yes, I think so, he replied. But I ask you, is it not possible for one loving not to be loved by him whom he loves? It is. But again, may he not be even hated while loving? This, I imagine, is the sort of thing that lovers do sometimes seem to incur with their favorites:

greek_head: ὡς οἷόν τε μάλιστα οἱ μὲν οἴονται οὐκ ἀντιφιλεῖσθαι οἱ δὲ καὶ μισεῖσθαι ἢ οὐκ ἀληθὲς δοκεῖ σοι τοῦτο
greek_tokens: ws oios te malista e men oiomai ou antifilew e de kai misew h ou alhqhs dokew su outos sfodra ge fhmi alhqhs oukoun en o toioutos eimi de egw o men filew o de filew nai poteros oun autos poteros filos eimi o filos o filew ean kai antifilew ean kai misew h o filew o filew h oudeteros au en o toioutos oudeteros filos eimi an mh amfoteros allhlwn filew eoika goun
english_head: they love them with all their might, yet they feel either that they are not loved in return, or that they are actually hated. Or do you not think this is true? Very true, he replied. Now in such a case, I went on, the one loves and the other is loved? Yes. Which of the two, then, is a friend of the other? Is the loving a friend of the loved, whether in fact he is loved in return or is even hated, or is the loved a friend of the loving? Or again, is neither of them in such a case friend of the other, if both do not love mutually?

greek_head: οὕτως ἔχειν Ἀλλοίως ἄρα νῦν ἡμῖν δοκεῖ ἢ πρότερον ἔδοξεν τότε μὲν γάρ εἰ ὁ ἕτερος φιλοῖ φίλω εἶναι ἄμφω
greek_tokens: outws exw alloios ara nun egw dokew h proteros dokew tote men gar ei o eteros filew filos eimi amfw nun de an mh amfoteros filew oudeteros filos kinduneuw fhmi ou ara eimi filos o filew oudeis mh ou antifilew ou eoika oude ara filippos eimi os an e ippos mh antifilew oude filortuc oude au filokunes ge kai filoinos kai filogumnasths kai filosofos an mh o sofia autos antifilew h filew men outos
english_head: At any rate, he said, it looks as if this were so. So you see, we now hold a different view from what we held before. At first we said that if one of them loved, both were friends: but now, if both do not love, neither is a friend. It looks like it, he said. So there is no such thing as a friend for the lover who is not loved in return. Apparently not. And so we find no horse-lovers where the horses do not love in return, no quail-lovers, dog-lovers, wine-lovers, or sport-lovers on such terms, nor any lovers of wisdom if she returns not their love. Or does each person love these things,

greek_head: ἕκαστοι οὐ μέντοι φίλα ὄντα ἀλλὰ ψεύδεθ' ὁ ποιητής ὃς ἔφη
greek_tokens: ekastos ou mentoi filos eimi alla yeudw o poihths os fhmi olbios os pais te filos kai mwnuc ippos kai kuwn agreuths kai cenos allodapos ou egw dokew eimi de os alla alhqhs dokew legw su nai o filew ara o filew filos eimi ws eoika eimi menecenos ean filew ean kai misew oios kai o newsti gignomai paidia o men oudepw filew o
english_head: while yet failing to make friends of them, and was it a lying poet who said—Happy to have your children as friends, and your trampling horses,Scent-snuffing hounds, and a host when you travel abroad?Solon 21.2. I do not think so, he said. But do you think he spoke the truth? Yes. Then the loved object is a friend to the lover, it would seem, Menexenus, alike whether it loves or hates: for instance, new-born children,

greek_head: δὲ καὶ μισοῦντα ὅταν κολάζηται ὑπὸ τῆς μητρὸς ἢ ὑπὸ τοῦ πατρός ὅμως καὶ μισοῦντα ἐν ἐκείνῳ τῷ χρόνῳ πάντων
greek_tokens: de kai misew otan kolazw upo o mhthr h upo o pathr omws kai misew en ekeinos o xronos pas malista eimi o gonaw filtatos egw dokew fhmi outws exw ou ara o filos filos ek outos o logos alla o filew eoika kai o misew exqros ara alla ou o misos fainw polus ara upo o exqra filew upo de o filos misew kai o
english_head: who have either not begun to love, or already hate, if punished by their mother or their father, are yet at that very moment, and in spite of their hate, especially and pre-eminently friends to their parents. I think, he said, that is the case. Then this argument shows that it is not the lover who is a friend, but the loved. Apparently. And it is the hated who is an enemy, not the hater. Evidently. Then people must often be loved by their enemies, and hated by their friends, and be friends to their enemies and enemies to their friends,

greek_head: μὲν ἐχθροῖς φίλοι εἰσίν τοῖς δὲ φίλοις ἐχθροί εἰ τὸ φιλούμενον φίλον ἐστὶν ἀλλὰ μὴ τὸ φιλοῦν καίτοι πολλὴ
greek_tokens: men exqros filos eimi o de filos exqros ei o filew filos eimi alla mh o filew kaitoi polus alogia eimi filos etairos mallon de oimh kai adunatos o te filos exqros kai o exqros filos eimi alhqhs fhmi eoika legw eimi swkraths oukoun ei outos adunatos o filew an eaw filos o filew fainw o misew ara palin exqros o misew anagkh oukoun tautos egw sumbainw anagkaion eimi omologew
english_head: if the loved object is a friend rather than the loving agent. And yet it is a gross absurdity, my dear friend—I should say rather, an impossibility—that one should be an enemy to one’s friend and a friend to one’s enemy. You appear to be right there, Socrates, he said. Then if that is impossible, it is the loving that must be a friend of the loved. Evidently. And so the hating, on the other hand, will be an enemy of the hated. Necessarily. Hence in the end we shall find ourselves compelled to agree

greek_head: ἅπερ ἐπὶ τῶν πρότερον πολλάκις φίλον εἶναι μὴ φίλου πολλάκις δὲ καὶ ἐχθροῦ ὅταν ἢ μὴ φιλοῦν τις φιλῇ ἢ καὶ
greek_tokens: o epi o proteros pollakis filos eimi mh filos pollakis de kai exqros otan h mh filew tis filew h kai misew filew pollakis de exqros eimi mh exqros h kai filos otan h mh misew tis misew h kai filew misew kinduneuw fhmi tis oun dh xraw eimi de egw ei mhte e filew filos eimi mhte e filew mhte e filew te kai filew alla kai para outos allos tis eti fhmi eimi filos allhlwn gignomai ou ma o dios fhmi eimi swkraths ou panu euporew egw
english_head: to the same statement as we made before, that frequently a man is a friend of one who is no friend, and frequently even of an enemy, when he loves one who loves not, or even hates; while frequently a man may be an enemy of one who is no enemy or even a friend, when he hates one who hates not, or even loves. It looks like it, he said. What then are we to make of it, I asked, if neither the loving are to be friends, nor the loved, nor both the loving and loved together? For apart from these, are there any others left for us to cite as becoming friends to one another? For my part, Socrates, he said, I declare I can see no sort of shift.

greek_head: Ἆρα μή ἦν δ' ἐγώ ὦ Μενέξενε τὸ παράπαν οὐκ ὀρθῶς ἐζητοῦμεν Οὐκ ἔμοιγε δοκεῖ ὦ Σώκρατες ἔφη ὁ Λύσις
greek_tokens: ara mh eimi de egw eimi menecenos o parapan ou orqos zhtew ou egw dokew eimi swkraths fhmi o lusis kai ama eipon eruqriaw dokew gar egw akwn autos ekfeugw o legw dia o sfodra prosexw o new o legw dhlos de eimi kai oste akroaomai outws exw egw oun boulomai o te menecenos anapauw kai ekeinos hdomai o filosofia outws metaballw pros o
english_head: Can it be, Menexenus, I asked, that all through there has been something wrong with our inquiry? I think there has, Socrates, said Lysis, and blushed as soon as he said it; for it struck me that the words escaped him unintentionally, through his closely applying his mind to our talk—as he had noticeably done all the time he was listening. So then, as I wanted to give Menexenus a rest, and was delighted with the other’s taste for philosophy, I took occasion to shift the discussion over to Lysis, and said:

greek_head: Λύσιν ἐποιούμην τοὺς λόγους καὶ εἶπον Ὦ Λύσι ἀληθῆ μοι δοκεῖς λέγειν ὅτι εἰ ὀρθῶς ἡμεῖς ἐσκοποῦμεν οὐκ ἄν
greek_tokens: lusis poiew o logos kai eipon eimi lusis alhqhs egw dokew legw ostis ei orqos egw skopaw ou an pote outws planaw alla outos men mhketi eimi kai gar xalepos tis egw fainw wsper odos o skeyis h de terpw dokew egw xrh eimi skopaw o kata
english_head: Lysis, I think your remark is true, that if we were inquiring correctly we could never have gone so sadly astray. Well, let us follow our present line no further, since our inquiry looks to me a rather hard sort of path: I think we had best make for the point where we turned off,

greek_head: τοὺς ποιητάς οὗτοι γὰρ ἡμῖν ὥσπερ πατέρες τῆς σοφίας εἰσὶν καὶ ἡγεμόνες λέγουσι δὲ δήπου οὐ φαύλως ἀποφαινόμενοι
greek_tokens: o poihths outos gar egw wsper pathr o sofia eimi kai hgemwn legw de dhpou ou faulos apofainw peri o filos e tugxanw eimi alla o qeos autos fhmi poiew filos autos agw para allhlwn legw de pws outos ws oimh wdi aei su o omoios agos qeos ws o omoios
english_head: and be guided by the poets; for they are our fathers, as it were, and conductors in wisdom. They, of course, express themselves in no mean sort on the subject of friends, where they happen to be found; even saying that God himself makes them friends by drawing them to each other. The way they put it, I believe, is something like this:Yea, ever like and like together God doth draw,Hom. Od. 17.218

greek_head: καὶ ποιεῖ γνώριμον ἢ οὐκ ἐντετύχηκας τούτοις τοῖς ἔπεσιν Ἔγωγ' ἔφη Οὐκοῦν καὶ τοῖς τῶν σοφωτάτων συγγράμμασιν
greek_tokens: kai poiew gnwrimos h ou entugxanw outos o epos egw fhmi oukoun kai o o sofos suggramma entugxanw outos autos legw ostis o omoios o omoios anagkh aei filos eimi eimi de pou outos e peri fusis te kai o oloc dialegw kai grafw alhqhs fhmi legw ara oun eimi de egw eu legw isos fhmi isos eimi de egw o hmisus autos isos de kai pas alla egw ou sunihmi dokew gar egw o ge ponhros
english_head: and so brings them acquainted; or have you not come across these verses? Yes, I have, he replied. And you have also come across those writings of eminent sages, which tell us this very thing—that like must needs be always friend to like? I refer, of course, to those who debate or write about nature and the universe. Quite so, he said. Well now, I went on, are they right in what they say? Perhaps, he replied. Perhaps in one half of it, I said; perhaps in even the whole; only we do not comprehend it. We suppose that the nearer a wicked man

greek_head: τῷ πονηρῷ ὅσῳ ἂν ἐγγυτέρω προσίῃ καὶ μᾶλλον ὁμιλῇ τοσούτῳ ἐχθίων γίγνεσθαι ἀδικεῖ γάρ ἀδικοῦντας δὲ καὶ
greek_tokens: o ponhros osos an eggus proseimi kai mallon omilew tosoutos exqos gignomai adikew gar adikew de kai adikew adunatos pou filos eimi ou outws nai eimi de os outos men an toinun o legw o hmisus ou alhqhs eaw eiper e ponhros allhlwn omoios alhqhs legw alla egw dokew legw o agaqos omoios eimi allhlwn kai filos o de kakos osper kai legw peri autos mhdepote omoios mhde autos eautou eimi alla
english_head: approaches to a wicked man, and the more he consorts with him, the more hateful he becomes; for he injures him, and we consider it impossible that injurer and injured should be friends. Is it not so? Yes, he answered. On this showing, therefore, half of the saying cannot be true, if the wicked are like one another. Quite so. What I believe they mean is that the good are like one another, and are friends, while the bad—as is also said of them—are never like even their own selves,

greek_head: ἐμπλήκτους τε καὶ ἀσταθμήτους ὃ δὲ αὐτὸ αὑτῷ ἀνόμοιον εἴη καὶ διάφορον σχολῇ γέ τῳ ἄλλῳ ὅμοιον ἢ φίλον γένοιτ'
greek_tokens: emplhktos te kai astaqmhtos o de autos eautou anomoios eaw kai diaforos sxolazw ge tis allos omoios h filos gignomai an h ou kai sos dokew outws egw fhmi outos toinun ainissomai ws egw dokew eimi etairos e o omoios o omoios filos legw ws o agaqos o agaqos monos monos filos o de kakos oute agaqos oute kakazw oudepote eis alhqhs filios erxomai sundokew su kataneuw exw ara hdh tinw eimi e filos o gar logos egw
english_head: being so ill-balanced and unsteady; and when a thing is unlike itself and variable it can hardly become like or friend to anything else. You must surely agree to that? I do, he said. Hence I conclude there is a hidden meaning, dear friend, intended by those who say that like is friend to like, namely that the good alone is friend to the good alone, while the bad never enters into true friendship with either good or bad. Do you agree? He nodded assent.

greek_head: σημαίνει ὅτι οἳ ἂν ὦσιν ἀγαθοί Πάνυ γε ἔφη δοκεῖ Καὶ ἐμοί ἦν δ' ἐγώ καίτοι δυσχεραίνω τί γε ἐν αὐτῷ
greek_tokens: shmainw ostis e an eimi agaqos panu ge fhmi dokew kai egw eimi de egw kaitoi dusxerainw tis ge en autos ferw oun eimi pros zeus eidon tis kai upopteuw o omoios o omoios kaqa osos omoios filos kai eimi xrhsimos o toioutos o toioutos mallon de wde otioun omoios ostis omoios tis wfeleia exw h tis blabh an poiew dunamai o mh kai autos eautou h tis an pasxw o mh kai ufh
english_head: So now we can tell what friends are; since our argument discloses that they are any persons who may be good. I quite think so, said he. And I also, said I; and yet there is a point in it that makes me uneasy: so come, in Heaven’s name, let us make out what it is that I suspect. Is like friend to like in so far as he is like, and is such an one useful to his fellow? Let me put it another way: when anything whatever is like anything else, what benefit can it offer, or what harm can it do, to its like, which it could not offer or do to itself? Or what could be done to it that could not be done to it by itself?

greek_head: αὑτοῦ πάθοι τὰ δὴ τοιαῦτα πῶς ἂν ὑπ' ἀλλήλων ἀγαπηθείη μηδεμίαν ἐπικουρίαν ἀλλήλοις ἔχοντα ἔστιν ὅπως Οὐκ
greek_tokens: eautou pasxw o dh toioutos pws an upo allhlwn agapaw mhdeis epikouria allhlwn exw eimi opws ou eimi o de mh agapaw pws filos oudamos alla dh o men omoios o omoios ou filos o de agaqos o agaqos kaqa osos agaqos ou kaqa osos omoios filos an eaw isos tis de ou o agaqos kaqa osos agaqos kata tosoutos ikanos an eaw eautou nai o de ge ikanos oudeis deomai kata o ikanoths pws gar ou o de mh tis
english_head: How can such things be cherished by each other, when they can bring no mutual succor? Is it at all possible? No. And how can that be a friend, which is not cherished? By no means. But, granting that like is not friend to like, the good may still be friend to the good in so far as he is good, not as he is like? Perhaps. But again, will not the good, in so far as he is good, be in that measure sufficient for himself? Yes. And the sufficient has no need of anything,

greek_head: δεόμενος οὐδέ τι ἀγαπῴη ἄν Οὐ γὰρ οὖν Ὃ δὲ μὴ ἀγαπῴη οὐδ' ἂν φιλοῖ Οὐ δῆτα Ὁ δὲ μὴ φιλῶν γε οὐ
greek_tokens: deomai oude tis agapaw an ou gar oun o de mh agapaw oude an filew ou dhta o de mh filos ge ou filos ou fainw pws oun e agaqos o agaqos egw filos eimi o arxh e mhte apeimi poqeinos allhlwn ikanos gar eautou kai xwris eimi mhte pareimi xreia eautou exw o dh toioutos tis mhxanh peri polus poiew allhlwn oudeis fhmi filos
english_head: by virtue of his sufficiency. Of course. And if a man has no need of anything he will not cherish anything. Presumably not. And that which does not cherish will not love. I should think not. And one who loves not is no friend. Evidently. So how can we say that the good will be friends to the good at all, when neither in absence do they long for one another—for they are sufficient for themselves even when apart—nor in presence have they need of one another? How can it be contrived that such persons shall value each other highly? By no means, he said.

greek_head: δέ γε οὐκ ἂν εἶεν μὴ περὶ πολλοῦ ποιούμενοι ἑαυτούς Ἀληθῆ
greek_tokens: de ge ou an eien mh peri polus poiew eautou alhqhs aqrew dh eimi lusis ph parakrouw ara ge oloc tis ecapataw pws dh fhmi hdh pote tis akouw legw kai arti anamimnhskw ostis o men omoios o omoios kai e agaqos o agaqos polemios eien kai dh kai o hsiodos epagw martus legos ws ara kai kerameus kerameus kotaw kai aoidos aoidaw
english_head: And if they do not set a high value on each other, they cannot be friends. True. Now observe, Lysis, how we are missing the track. Can it be, indeed, that we are deceived in the whole matter? How so? he asked. Once on a time I heard somebody say, and I have just recollected it, that like was most hostile to like, and so were good men to good men; and what is more, he put forward Hesiod as witness, by quoting his words—See potter wroth with potter, bard with bard,

greek_head: καὶ πτωχὸς πτωχῷ καὶ τἆλλα δὴ πάντα οὕτως ἔφη ἀναγκαῖον εἶναι μάλιστα τὰ
greek_tokens: kai ptwxos ptwxos kai allos dh pas outws fhmi anagkaion eimi malista o omoios pros allhlwn fqonos te kai filoneikia kai exqra empiplhmi o de anomoios filios o gar penhs o plousios anagkazw filos eimi kai o asqenhs o isxuros o epikouria eneka kai o kamnw o iatros kai pas dh o mh oida agaph o oida kai filew
english_head: Beggar with beggar,Hes. WD 25and in all other cases it was the same, he said; likest things must needs be filled with envy, contention, and hatred against each other, but the unlikest things with friendship: since the poor man must needs be friendly to the rich, and the weak to the strong, for the sake of assistance, and also the sick man to the doctor; and every ignorant person had to cherish the well-informed, and love him. And then the speaker pursued his theme to this further and more imposing point—that like could not in the slightest degree be friendly to like,

greek_head: καὶ δὴ καὶ ἔτι ἐπεξῄει τῷ λόγῳ μεγαλοπρεπέστερον λέγων ὡς ἄρα παντὸς δέοι τὸ ὅμοιον τῷ ὁμοίῳ φίλον εἶναι ἀλλ'
greek_tokens: kai dh kai eti epeceimi o logos megaloprephs legos ws ara pas dew o omoios o omoios filos eimi alla autos o enantion eaw outos o gar enantios o enantios eimi malista filos epiqumew gar o toioutos ekastos alla ou o omoios o men gar chros ugros o de yuxros qermos o de pikros glukus o de ocus amblus o de kenos plhrwsis kai o plhrhs de kenwsis kai allos outws kata o autos logos trofh gar eimi o enantion o enantios o gar omoios o omoios
english_head: but was in just the opposite case: for it was between things most opposed that friendship was chiefly to be found, since everything desired its opposite, not its like. Thus dry desired wet, cold hot, bitter sweet, sharp blunt, empty fullness, full emptiness, and likewise the rest on the same principle: for the opposite was food for its opposite, as the like

greek_head: οὐδὲν ἂν ἀπολαῦσαι καὶ μέντοι ὦ ἑταῖρε καὶ κομψὸς ἐδόκει εἶναι ταῦτα λέγων εὖ γὰρ ἔλεγεν ὑμῖν δέ ἦν δ'
greek_tokens: oudeis an apolauw kai mentoi eimi etairos kai komyos dokew eimi outos legos eu gar legw su de eimi de egw pws dokew legw eu ge fhmi o menecenos os ge outwsi akew fhmi ara o enantion o enantios malista filos eimi panu ge eien eimi de egw ou allokotos eimi menecenos kai egw euqus asmenos epiphdaw outos e pansofos anhr e antilogikos kai eromai ei
english_head: could have no enjoyment of its like. And I must say, my good friend, his argument seemed a smart one, for he expressed it well. But you, I asked—how does it strike you? It sounds all right, said Menexenus, at least on the moment’s hearing. Then are we to say that the opposite is most friendly to its opposite? Certainly. Well, I exclaimed, is it not monstrous, Menexenus? Why, at once these all-accomplished logic-choppers will delightedly pounce on us and ask whether hatred is not the most opposite thing to friendship.

greek_head: οὐκ ἐναντιώτατον ἔχθρα φιλίᾳ οἷς τί ἀποκρινούμεθα ἢ οὐκ ἀνάγκη ὁμολογεῖν ὅτι ἀληθῆ λέγουσιν Ἀνάγκη Ἆρ'
greek_tokens: ou enantios exqra filios os tis apokrinw h ou anagkh omologew ostis alhqhs legw anagkh ara oun fhmi o exqros o filos filos h o filos o exqros oudeteros fhmi alla o dikaios o adikos h o swfrwn o akolastos h o agaqos o kakazw ou an egw dokew outws exw alla mentoi eimi de egw eiper ge kata o enantioths tis tis filos filos eimi anagkh kai outos filos eimi anagkh oute ara o omoios o omoios oute o enantion o enantios filos ou eoika
english_head: And what answer shall we give them? Shall we not be forced to admit that what they say is true? We shall. So then, they will demand, is a hating thing friend to the friendly thing, or the friendly to the hating? Neither, he replied. But is the just a friend to the unjust, or the temperate to the profligate, or the good to the bad? I do not think that could be so. But yet, I urged, if one thing is friend to another on this principle of opposition, these things too must needs be friends. They must. So neither is like friend to like, nor opposite friend to opposite. It seems not.

greek_head: Ἔτι δὲ καὶ τόδε σκεψώμεθα μὴ ἔτι μᾶλλον ἡμᾶς λανθάνει τὸ φίλον ὡς ἀληθῶς οὐδὲν τούτων ὄν ἀλλὰ τὸ μήτε
greek_tokens: eti de kai ode skeptomai mh eti mallon egw lanqanw o filos ws alhqhs oudeis outos eimi alla o mhte agaqos mhte kakos filos outws pote gignomai o agaqos pws eimi de os legw alla ma dios eimi de egw ou oida alla o eimi autos eiliggiaw upo o o logos aporia kai kinduneuw kata o arxaios paroimia o kalos filos eimi eoika goun malakos tis kai leios kai liparos
english_head: But there is still this point to consider; for perhaps we are yet more mistaken, and the friendly has really nothing to do with all this: it may rather be something neither good nor bad that will prove after all to be what we call friend of the good. How do you mean? he asked. For the life of me, I said, I cannot tell: the fact is, I am quite dizzy myself with the puzzle of our argument, and am inclined to agree with the ancient proverb that the beautiful is friendly. It certainly resembles something soft and smooth and sleek;

greek_head: διὸ καὶ ἴσως ῥᾳδίως διολισθαίνει καὶ διαδύεται ἡμᾶς ἅτε τοιοῦτον ὄν λέγω γὰρ τἀγαθὸν καλὸν εἶναι σὺ δ' οὐκ
greek_tokens: dio kai isos radios diolisqanw kai diadunw egw ate toioutos eimi legos gar agaqos kalos eimi su de ou oiomai egw legos toinun apomanteuomai o kalew te kai agaqos filos eimi o mhte agaqos mhte kakos pros o de legos manteuomai akouw dokew egw wsper treis atta eimi genos o men agaqos o de kakos o de oute agaqos oute kakos tis de sos kai egw fhmi kai oute agaqos agaqos oute o kakos o kakazw oute agaqos o
english_head: that is why, I daresay, it so easily slides and dives right into us, by virtue of those qualities. For I declare that the good is beautiful: do you not agree? I do. Then I will be a diviner for once, and state that what is neither good nor bad is friendly to what is beautiful and good; and what it is that prompts me to this divination, you must now hear. My view is that there are three separate kinds, as it were—the good, the bad, and what is neither good nor bad; and what is yours? Mine is the same, he replied.

greek_head: κακῷ φίλον εἶναι ὥσπερ οὐδ' ὁ ἔμπροσθεν λόγος ἐᾷ λείπεται δή εἴπερ τῴ τί ἐστιν φίλον τὸ μήτε ἀγαθὸν μήτε
greek_tokens: kakazw filos eimi wsper oude o emprosqen logos eaw leipw dh eiper tis tis eimi filos o mhte agaqos mhte kakos filos eimi h o agaqos h o toioutos oios autos eimi ou gar an pou o kakazw filos an tis gignomai alhqhs oude meis o omoios o omoios fhmi arti eimi gar nai ou ara eimi o mhte agaqos mhte kakazw o toioutos filos oios autos ou fainw o agaqos ara
english_head: And that neither is the good friendly to the good, nor the bad to the bad, nor the good to the bad; so much our previous argument already forbids. One view then remains: if anything is friendly to anything, that which is neither good nor bad is friendly to either the good or what is of the same quality as itself. For I presume nothing could be found friendly to the bad. True. Nor, however, can like be friendly to like: this we stated just now, did we not? Yes. So what is neither good nor bad can have no friendship with the same sort of thing as itself. Apparently not. Then only what is neither good nor bad proves to be friendly to the good,

greek_head: τὸ μήτε ἀγαθὸν μήτε κακὸν μόνῳ μόνον συμβαίνει γίγνεσθαι φίλον Ἀνάγκη ὡς ἔοικεν
greek_tokens: o mhte agaqos mhte kakos monos monos sumbainw gignomai filos anagkh ws eoika ara oun kai kalos eimi de egw eimi pais ufhgeomai egw o nun legw ei goun eqelw ennoew o ugiainw swma oudeis iatrikos deomai oude wfeleia ikanos gar exis wste ugiainw oudeis iatros filos dia o ugieia eimi gar oudeis alla o kamnw oimh dia o nosos pws gar
english_head: and to that only. That must be so, it seems. Then can we rely further on this present statement, my boys, I said, as a sure guide? For instance, we have only to consider a body in health to see that it has no need of doctoring or assistance: it is well enough as it is, and so no one in health is friend to a doctor, on account of his health. You agree? Yes. But the sick man is, I imagine, on account of his disease. Certainly. Now disease is a bad thing, and medicine is beneficial and good. Yes. And a body, of course, taken as body, is neither good nor bad.

greek_head: οὔ Νόσος μὲν δὴ κακόν ἰατρικὴ δὲ ὠφέλιμον καὶ ἀγαθόν Ναί Σῶμα δέ γέ που κατὰ τὸ σῶμα εἶναι οὔτε ἀγαθὸν
greek_tokens: ou nosos men dh kakos iatrikos de wfelimos kai agaqos nai swma de ge pou kata o swma eimi oute agaqos oute kakos outws anagkazw de ge swma dia nosos iatrikos aspazomai kai filew dokew egw o mhte kakos ara mhte agaqos filos gignomai o agaqos dia kakos parousia eoika dhlos de ge ostis prin gignomai autos kakos upo o kakos e exis ou gar dh ge kakos gignomai
english_head: That is so. But a body is compelled by disease to welcome and love medicine. I think so. Thus what is neither bad nor good becomes a friend of the good because of the presence of evil. So it seems. But clearly this must be before it is itself made evil by the evil which it has; for surely, when once it has been made evil, it can no longer have any desire or love for the good; since we agreed it was impossible

greek_head: ἔτι ἄν τι τοῦ ἀγαθοῦ οὗ ἐπιθυμοῖ καὶ φίλον εἴη ἀδύνατον γὰρ ἔφαμεν κακὸν ἀγαθῷ φίλον εἶναι Ἀδύνατον γάρ
greek_tokens: eti an tis o agaqos e epiqumew kai filos eaw adunatos gar fhmi kakos agaqos filos eimi adunatos gar skeptomai dh o legos legos gar ostis enioi men oios an eimi o pareimi toioutos eimi kai autos enioi de ou wsper ei eqelw tis xrwma tis otioun tis aleifw pareimi pou o aleifw o epaleifw panu ge ara oun kai eimi tote toioutos o xroia o aleifw oios o epeimi
english_head: for bad to be a friend of good. Yes, impossible. Now observe what I say. Some things are of the same sort as those that are present with them, and some are not. For example, if you chose to dye something a certain color, the substance of the dye is present, I presume, with the thing dyed. Certainly. Then is the thing dyed of the same sort, in point of color, as the substance that is added? I do not understand, he said.

greek_head: Οὐ μανθάνω ἦ δ' ὅς Ἀλλ' ὧδε ἦν δ' ἐγώ εἴ τίς σου ξανθὰς οὔσας τὰς τρίχας ψιμυθίῳ ἀλείψειεν πότερον
greek_tokens: ou manqanw eimi de os alla wde eimi de egw ei tis su canqos eimi o qric yimuqion aleifw poteros tote leukos eien h fainw an fainw an eimi de os kai meis pareimi ge an autos leukoths nai alla omws oudeis tis mallon an eien leukos pw alla pareimi leukoths oute tis leukos oute melaina eimi alhqhs alla otan dh eimi filos o ghras autos tautos outos xrwma epagw tote gignomai oionper o pareimi leukon parousia
english_head: Well, try it this way, I went on: suppose some one tinged your golden locks with white lead, would they then be or appear to be white? Yes, they would so appear, he replied. And, in fact, whiteness would be present with them? Yes. But all the same they would not be any the more white as yet; for though whiteness be present, they are not at all white, any more than they are at all black. True. But when, my dear boy, old age has cast that same color upon them, they have then come to be of the same sort as

greek_head: λευκαί Πῶς γὰρ οὔ Τοῦτο τοίνυν ἐρωτῶ νῦν δή εἰ ᾧ ἄν τι παρῇ τοιοῦτον ἔσται τὸ ἔχον οἷον τὸ παρόν ἢ ἐὰν
greek_tokens: leukos pws gar ou outos toinun erwtaw nun dh ei os an tis pareimi toioutos eimi o exw oios o pareimi h ean men kata tis tropos pareimi eimi ean de mh ou outws mallon fhmi kai o mhte kakos ara mhte agaqos eniote kakos pareimi oupw kakos eimi eimi de oste hdh o toioutos gignomai panu ge oukoun otan mhpw kakos eimi kakos pareimi outos men o parousia agaqos autos poiew epiqumew o de kakos poiew aposterew autos o te epiqumia ama kai o filios o agaqos ou gar eti eimi
english_head: that which is present—white through presence of white. To be sure. So this is the question I have been trying to put to you—whether a thing that has something present with it is to be held of the same sort as that present thing or only when that thing is present in a particular way, but otherwise not? More likely the latter, he said. So that what is neither bad nor good is sometimes, when bad is present, not bad as yet, and such cases have been known to occur. Certainly. When therefore it is not bad as yet, though bad is present, this presence makes it desire good; but the presence which makes it bad deprives it equally of its desire and its love for the good. For it is no longer

greek_head: οὔτε κακὸν οὔτε ἀγαθόν ἀλλὰ κακόν φίλον δὲ ἀγαθῷ κακὸν οὐκ ἦν Οὐ γὰρ οὖν Διὰ ταῦτα δὴ φαῖμεν ἂν καὶ τοὺς
greek_tokens: oute kakos oute agaqos alla kakos filos de agaqos kakos ou eimi ou gar oun dia outos dh fhmi an kai o hdh sofos mhketi filosofew eite qeos eite anqrwpos eimi outos oude au ekeinos filosofew o outws agnoia exw wste kakos eimi kakos gar kai amaqhs oudeis filosofew leipw dh e exw men o kakos outos o agnoia mhpw de upo autos eimi agnwmwn mhde
english_head: neither bad nor good, but bad; and we found that bad was no friend to good. No, indeed. And consequently we may say that those who are already wise no longer love wisdom, whether they be gods or men; nor again can those be lovers of wisdom who are in such ignorance as to be bad: for we know that a bad and stupid man is no lover of wisdom. And now there remain those who, while possessing this bad thing, ignorance, are not yet made ignorant or stupid, but are still aware of not knowing the things

greek_head: ἀμαθεῖς ἀλλ' ἔτι ἡγούμενοι μὴ εἰδέναι ἃ μὴ ἴσασιν διὸ δὴ καὶ φιλοσοφοῦσιν οἱ οὔτε ἀγαθοὶ οὔτε κακοί πω ὄντες
greek_tokens: amaqhs alla eti hgeomai mh oida o mh oida dio dh kai filosofew e oute agaqos oute kakos pw eimi osos de kakos ou filosofew oude e agaqos oute gar o enantion o enantios oute o omoios o omoios filos egw fainw en o emprosqen logos h ou mimnhskw panu ge fhmi nun ara eimi de egw eimi lusis te kai menecenos pas mallon eceuriskw o eimi o filos kai ou fhmi gar autos kai kata o yuxh kai kata o
english_head: they do not know. It follows, then, that those who are as yet neither good nor bad are lovers of wisdom, while all who are bad, and all the good, are not: for, as we found in our previous discussion, neither is opposite friend to opposite, nor like to like. You remember, do you not? To be sure we do, they both replied. So now, Lysis and Menexenus, I said, we can count on having discovered what is the friendly and what is not. For we say that, in the soul

greek_head: σῶμα καὶ πανταχοῦ τὸ μήτε κακὸν μήτε ἀγαθὸν διὰ κακοῦ παρουσίαν τοῦ ἀγαθοῦ φίλον εἶναι Παντάπασιν ἐφάτην τε
greek_tokens: swma kai pantaxou o mhte kakos mhte agaqos dia kakos parousia o agaqos filos eimi pantapasi fhmi te kai sugxwrew outws outos exw kai dh kai autos egw panu xairw wsper qhreuths tis exw agaphtos o qhreuw apeimi ou oida opoqen egw atopos tis upoyia eiserxomai ws ou alhqhs eaw o omologew egw kai euqus axqesqeis eipon babai eimi lusis te kai menecenos kinduneuw onar ploutew
english_head: and the body and everywhere, just that which is neither bad nor good, but has the presence of bad, is thereby friend of the good. To this statement they said that they entirely agreed. And, beyond that, I was myself filled with delight, like a hunter, at the satisfaction of getting hold of what I was hunting; when somehow or other a most unaccountable suspicion came over me that the conclusion to which we had agreed was not true. So at once I exclaimed in vexation: Alack-a-day, Lysis and Menexenus! I fear our new-gotten riches are all a dream.

greek_head: Τί μάλιστα ἔφη ὁ Μενέξενος Φοβοῦμαι ἦν δ' ἐγώ μὴ ὥσπερ ἀνθρώποις ἀλαζόσιν
greek_tokens: tis malista fhmi o menecenos fobew eimi de egw mh wsper anqrwpos alazwn logos tis toioutos yeudhs entugxanw peri o filos pws dh fhmi wde eimi de egw skopaw filos os an eaw poteros eimi tis filos h ou anagkh fhmi poteros oun oudeis eneka kai dia oudeis h eneka tis kai dia tis eneka tis kai dia tis poteros filos eimi ekeinos o pragma e eneka filos o filos o filos h oute filos oute exqros
english_head: How on earth is that? said Menexenus. I am afraid, I replied, that in our search for friendship we have struck up with arguments that are no better than a set of braggarts. How so? he asked. Just consider a moment, I said. When a man is a friend, is he friend to some one or not? He needs be, he replied. Then is he so for the sake of nothing and because of nothing, or for the sake of something and because of something? For the sake of something, and because of something. Is it a friend—that thing for whose sake he is a friend to his friend—or is it neither friend nor foe? I do not quite follow, he said.

greek_head: Οὐ πάνυ ἔφη ἕπομαι Εἰκότως γε ἦν δ' ἐγώ ἀλλ' ὧδε ἴσως ἀκολουθήσεις οἶμαι δὲ καὶ ἐγὼ μᾶλλον εἴσομαι
greek_tokens: ou panu fhmi epomai eoikotws ge eimi de egw alla wde isos akolouqhsis oimh de kai egw mallon eimi ostis legos o kamnw nundi fhmi o iatros filos ou outws nai oukoun dia nosos eneka ugieia o iatros filos nai o de ge nosos kakos pws de ou tis de ugieia eimi de egw agaqos h kakos h oudeteros agaqos
english_head: Naturally enough, said I; but perhaps you will keep up if we try it another way, and I expect that I too will better understand what I am saying. The sick man, we said just now, is a friend to the doctor; is not that so? Yes. Then is it because of disease, for the sake of health, that he is a friend of the doctor? Yes. And disease is a bad thing? Of course. But what is health? I asked: a good thing, or a bad, or neither? A good thing, he said.

greek_head: ἔφη Ἐλέγομεν δ' ἄρα ὡς ἔοικεν ὅτι τὸ σῶμα οὔτε ἀγαθὸν οὔτε κακὸν ὄν διὰ τὴν νόσον τοῦτο δὲ διὰ τὸ κακόν
greek_tokens: fhmi legw de ara ws eoika ostis o swma oute agaqos oute kakos eimi dia o nosos outos de dia o kakos o iatrikos filos eimi agaqos de iatrikos eneka de o ugieia o filios o iatrikos anairew o de ugieia agaqos eimi gar nai filos de h ou filos o ugieia filos o de nosos exqros panu ge o oute kakos oute
english_head: And we were saying, I believe, that the body, being neither good nor bad, was a friend of medicine—that is, of a good thing—because of disease—that is, because of a bad thing; and it is for the sake of health that medicine has acquired this friendship, and health is a good thing. You agree? Yes. Is health a friend or not? A friend. And disease is a foe? Certainly. So what is neither bad nor good

greek_head: ἀγαθὸν ἄρα διὰ τὸ κακὸν καὶ τὸ ἐχθρὸν τοῦ ἀγαθοῦ φίλον ἐστὶν ἕνεκα τοῦ ἀγαθοῦ καὶ φίλου Φαίνεται Ἕνεκα ἄρα
greek_tokens: agaqos ara dia o kakos kai o exqros o agaqos filos eimi eneka o agaqos kai filos fainw eneka ara o filos o filos o filos filos dia o exqros eoika eien eimi de egw epei entauqa hkw eimi pais prosexw o new mh ecapataw ostis men gar filos o filos o filos gignomai eaw xairw kai o omoios ge o omoios filos gignomai o fhmi adunatos eimi alla omws ode skeptomai mh egw ecapathsis o nun legw
english_head: is a friend to the good because of what is bad and a foe for the sake of what is good and a friend. Apparently. Hence the friend is a friend of its friend for the sake of its friend and because of its foe. So it seems. Very well, I said: since we have reached this point, my boys, let us take good heed not to be deceived. I pass over without remark the fact that the friend has become a friend to the friend, and thus the like becomes a friend to the like, which we said was impossible. There is, however, a further point which we must examine,

greek_head: ἡ ἰατρική φαμέν ἕνεκα τῆς ὑγιείας φίλον Ναί Οὐκοῦν καὶ ἡ ὑγίεια φίλον Πάνυ γε Εἰ ἄρα φίλον
greek_tokens: o iatrikos fhmi eneka o ugieia filos nai oukoun kai o ugieia filos panu ge ei ara filos eneka tis nai filos ge tis dh eiper akolouqhsis o prosqen omologia panu ge oukoun kai ekeinos filos au eimi eneka filos nai ara oun ou anagkh apeipon egw outws eimi h afikneomai epi tis arxh o ouketi epanaferw epi allos filos alla hkw epi ekeinos o eimi
english_head: if we are not to find our present argument a mere deception. Medicine, we say, is a friend for the sake of health. Yes. Then is health a friend also? Certainly. And if it is a friend, it is so for the sake of something. Yes. And that something is a friend, if it is to conform to our previous agreement. Quite so. Then will that something be, on its part also, a friend for the sake of a friend? Yes. Now are we not bound to weary ourselves with going on in this way, unless we can arrive at some first principle which will not keep leading us on from one friend to another, but will reach the one original friend, for whose sake all the other things can be said

greek_head: πρῶτον φίλον οὗ ἕνεκα καὶ τὰ ἄλλα φαμὲν πάντα φίλα εἶναι Ἀνάγκη Τοῦτο δή ἐστιν ὃ λέγω μὴ ἡμᾶς τἆλλα
greek_tokens: proteros filos e eneka kai o allos fhmi pas filos eimi anagkh outos dh eimi o legos mh egw allos pas o eipon ekeinos eneka filos eimi wsper eidwlon atta eimi autos ecapataw eimi de ekeinos o proteros o ws alhqhs eimi filos ennoew gar outwsi otan tis tis peri polus poiew oionper eniote pathr uios anti pas o allos xrhma protimaw o dh toioutos eneka o o
english_head: to be friends? We must. So you see what I am afraid of—that all the other things, which we cited as friends for the sake of that one thing, may be deceiving us like so many phantoms of it, while that original thing may be the veritable friend. For suppose we view the matter thus: when a man highly values a thing, as in the common case of a father who prizes his son above all his possessions, will such a man, for the sake of placing his son before everything,

greek_head: ὑὸν περὶ παντὸς ἡγεῖσθαι ἆρα καὶ ἄλλο τι ἂν περὶ πολλοῦ ποιοῖτο οἷον εἰ αἰσθάνοιτο αὐτὸν κώνειον πεπωκότα ἆρα
greek_tokens: uios peri pas hgeomai ara kai allos tis an peri polus poiew oios ei aisqanomai autos kwneion pinw ara peri polus poiew an oinon eiper outos hgeomai o uios swzw tis meis fhmi oukoun kai o aggeion en os o oinos enihmi panu ge ara oun tote oudeis peri pleiwn poiew kulic kerameos h o uios o eautou oude trew kotulh oinon h o uios h wde pws exis pas o toioutos spoudh ou epi outos eimi spoudazw epi o eneka tis paraskeuazw alla epi ekeinos e eneka pas o
english_head: value anything else highly at the same time? For instance, on learning that he had drunk some hemlock, would he value wine highly if he believed it would save his son’s life? Why, of course, he said. And the vessel too which contained the wine? Certainly. Now does he make no distinction in value, at that moment, between a cup of earthenware and his own son, or between three pints of wine and his son? Or may we perhaps state it thus: all such concern is not entertained for the actual things which are applied for the sake of something, but for that something for whose sake all the rest are applied?

greek_head: τοιαῦτα παρασκευάζεται οὐχ ὅτι πολλάκις λέγομεν ὡς περὶ πολλοῦ ποιούμεθα χρυσίον καὶ ἀργύριον ἀλλὰ μὴ
greek_tokens: toioutos paraskeuazw ou ostis pollakis legw ws peri polus poiew xrusion kai argurion alla mh oudeis tis mallon outws o ge alhqhs exis alla ekeinos eimi o peri pas poiew o an fainw eimi ostis eneka kai xrusion kai pas o paraskeuazw paraskeuazw ara outws fhmi panu ge oukoun kai peri o filos o autos logos osos gar fhmi filos eimi egw eneka filos
english_head: I know that we often talk of setting great value on gold and silver: but surely we are no nearer the truth of the matter for that; what we rather value above everything is the thing—whatever it may prove to be—for whose sake gold and all the other commodities are applied. May we state it so? By all means. Then shall we not give the same account of a friend? In speaking of all the things that are friends to us

greek_head: τινὸς ἑτέρου ῥήματι φαινόμεθα λέγοντες αὐτό φίλον δὲ τῷ ὄντι κινδυνεύει ἐκεῖνο αὐτὸ εἶναι εἰς ὃ πᾶσαι αὗται αἱ
greek_tokens: tis eteros rhma fainw legw autos filos de o eimi kinduneuw ekeinos autos eimi eis o paomai outos o legw filios teleutaw kinduneuw outws fhmi exw oukoun o ge o eimi filos ou filos tis eneka filos eimi alhqhs outos men dh apallassw mh filos tis eneka o filos filos eimi alla ara o agaqos eimi filos egw dokew ara oun dia o kakos o agaqos filew
english_head: for the sake of some other friend, we find ourselves uttering a mere phrase; whereas in reality friend appears to be simply and solely the thing in which all these so-called friendships terminate. So it appears, he said. Then the real friend is a friend for the sake of nothing else that is a friend? True. So we have got rid of this, and it is not for the sake of some friendly thing that the friend is friendly. But now, is the good a friend? I should say so. And further, it is because of the bad that the good is loved ;

greek_head: καὶ ἔχει ὧδε εἰ τριῶν ὄντων ὧν νυνδὴ ἐλέγομεν ἀγαθοῦ καὶ κακοῦ καὶ μήτε ἀγαθοῦ μήτε κακοῦ τὰ δύο λειφθείη τὸ
greek_tokens: kai exis wde ei treis eimi os nundi legw agaqos kai kakos kai mhte agaqos mhte kakos o duo leipw o de kakos ekpodwn aperxomai kai mhdeis efaptw mhte swma mhte yuxazw mhte o allos o dh fhmi autos kaqa eautou oute kakos eimi oute agaqos ara tote oudeis an egw xrhsimos eaw o agaqos alla axrhstos an gignomai eaw ei gar mhdeis egw eti blaptw oudeis an oudeis
english_head: let me state the case as follows: there are three things of which we have just been speaking—good, bad, and what is neither good nor bad. If but two of these remained after evil had been cleared away, so that it had no contact with anything, whether body or soul or any of the other things that we count neither bad nor good in themselves, would the result be that good would be of no use to us, but would have become quite a useless thing? For if there were nothing left to harm us, we should feel no want

greek_head: ὠφελίας δεοίμεθα καὶ οὕτω δὴ ἂν τότε γένοιτο κατάδηλον ὅτι διὰ τὸ κακὸν τἀγαθὸν ἠγαπῶμεν καὶ ἐφιλοῦμεν ὡς
greek_tokens: wfeleia deomai kai outws dh an tote gignomai katadhlos ostis dia o kakos agaqos agapaw kai filew ws farmakon eimi o kakos o agaqos o de kakos noshma noshma de mh eimi oudeis deomai farmakon ara outws fuw te kai filew agaqos dia o kakos ufh egw o metacu eimi o kakos te kai agaqos autos de eautou eneka oudeis xreia exis eoika eimi de os outws exw o ara filos egw ekeinos eis o teleutaw pas o allos
english_head: of any assistance; and thus we should have to face the fact that it was because of the bad that we felt such a friendly affection for the good, since the good is a cure for the bad, while the bad is an ailment, and if there is no ailment there is no need for a cure. Is not this the nature of the good—to be loved because of the bad by us who are midway between the bad and the good, whereas separately and for its own sake it is of no use? Apparently so, he said. Then our friend, in which

greek_head: ἕνεκα ἑτέρου φίλου φίλα ἔφαμεν εἶναι ἐκεῖνα οὐδὲν δὲ τούτοις ἔοικεν ταῦτα μὲν γὰρ φίλου ἕνεκα φίλα κέκληται
greek_tokens: eneka eteros filos filos fhmi eimi ekeinos oudeis de outos eoika outos men gar filos eneka filos kalew o de o eimi filos pas enantion outos fainw fuw filos gar egw anafainw eimi exqros eneka ei de o exqros aperxomai ouketi ws eoika esqw egw filos ou egw dokew fhmi os ge nun legw poteros eimi de egw pros zeus ean o kakos apollumi oude peinaw eti eimi oude
english_head: all the other things terminated—we called them friends for the sake of some other friend—has no resemblance to these. For they are described as friends for the sake of a friend: but the real friend appears to have quite the opposite character; for we found if to be a friend for the sake of a foe, and if the foe should be removed we have no friend, it seems, any more. I should say not, he assented, to judge by our present argument. Tell me, I beg of you, I went on, if evil is abolished, will it be impossible any longer to feel hunger

greek_head: διψῆν οὐδὲ ἄλλο οὐδὲν τῶν τοιούτων ἢ πείνη μὲν ἔσται ἐάνπερ ἄνθρωποί τε καὶ τἆλλα ζῷα ᾖ οὐ μέντοι βλαβερά
greek_tokens: diyaw oude allos oudeis o toioutos h peina men eimi ean anqrwpos te kai allos zwion eimi ou mentoi blaberos ge kai diya dh kai o allos epiqumia alla ou kakos ate o kakos apollumi h geloios o erwthma ostis pote eimi tote h mh eimi tis gar oida alla oun ode ge oida ostis kai nun eimi peinaw blaptw eimi de kai wfelew eimi gar panu ge oukoun kai diyaw kai
english_head: or thirst or other such conditions? Or will hunger exist, so long as men and animals exist, but without being hurtful? Thirst, too, and all other desires—will these exist without being bad, because the bad will have been abolished? Or is this a ridiculous question—as to what will exist or not exist in such a case? For who can tell? Yet this, at all events, we do know—that, as things are now, it is possible for a man to feel hunger as a hurt, and also to be benefited by it. You agree? Certainly. And so, when a man feels thirst or any other desire of the sort,

greek_head: τῶν ἄλλων τῶν τοιούτων πάντων ἐπιθυμοῦντα ἔστιν ἐνίοτε μὲν ὠφελίμως ἐπιθυμεῖν ἐνίοτε δὲ βλαβερῶς ἐνίοτε δὲ
greek_tokens: o allos o toioutos pas epiqumew eimi eniote men wfelimos epiqumew eniote de blaberos eniote de mhdeteros sfodra ge oukoun ean apollumi o kakos o ge mh tugxanw eimi kakos tis proshkw o kakos sunapollumi oudeis eimi ara o mhte agaqos mhte kakos epiqumia kai ean apollumi o kakos fainw oios te oun eimi epiqumew kai eraw outos e epiqumew kai eraomai mh filew ou egw dokew eimi ara
english_head: he may have that desire sometimes with benefit, sometimes with harm, and sometimes with neither? Quite so. Now if evil things are abolished, is there any reason why the things that are not evil should be abolished along with the evil? None. So that those desires which are neither good nor bad will exist even when the bad things are abolished. Apparently. Now is it possible for a man, when he desires and loves, to have no friendly feeling towards that which he desires and loves? I think not. Thus certain things will continue to be friendly, it seems, when evil things are abolished. Yes.

greek_head: καὶ τῶν κακῶν ἀπολομένων ὡς ἔοικεν φίλ' ἄττα Ναί Οὐκ ἄν εἴ γε τὸ κακὸν αἴτιον ἦν τοῦ φίλον τι εἶναι οὐκ ἂν
greek_tokens: kai o kakh apollumi ws eoika filos atta nai ou an ei ge o kakos aitios eimi o filos tis eimi ou an eimi outos apollumi filos eteros eteros aitios gar apollumi adunatos pou eimi etai ekeinos eimi e eimi outos o aitios orqos legw oukoun omologew egw o filos filew tis kai dia tis kai oiomai tote ge dia o kakos o mhte agaqos mhte kakos o agaqos filew alhqhs
english_head: It cannot be that, if evil were the cause of a thing being friendly, one thing should be friendly to another when evil is abolished. For when a cause is abolished, that thing can no longer exist, I presume, which had this as its cause. You are right. Now we have agreed that the friend has a friendly feeling for something and because of something; and we supposed, just then, that it was because of evil that what was neither good nor bad loved the good.

greek_head: Νῦν δέ γε ὡς ἔοικε φαίνεται ἄλλη τις αἰτία τοῦ φιλεῖν τε καὶ φιλεῖσθαι Ἔοικεν Ἆρ' οὖν τῷ ὄντι ὥσπερ ἄρτι
greek_tokens: nun de ge ws eoika fainw allos tis aitios o filew te kai filew eoika ara oun o eimi wsper arti legw o epiqumia o filios aitios kai o epiqumew filos eimi outos e epiqumew kai tote otan epiqumew o de o proteros legw filos eimi uqlos tis eimi wsper poihma makros sugkeimai kinduneuw fhmi alla mentoi eimi de egw o ge epiqumew e an endehs eimi outos
english_head: True. But now, it seems, we make out a different cause of loving and being loved. It seems so. Can it really be then, as we were saying just now, that desire is the cause of friendship, and the desiring thing is a friend to what which it desires, and is so at any time of desiring; while our earlier statement about friends was all mere drivel, like a poem strung out for mere length? It looks like it, he said. But still, I went on, the desiring thing desires

greek_head: ἐπιθυμεῖ ἦ γάρ Ναί Τὸ δ' ἐνδεὲς ἄρα φίλον ἐκείνου οὗ ἂν ἐνδεὲς ᾖ Δοκεῖ μοι Ἐνδεὲς δὲ γίγνεται οὗ ἄν τι
greek_tokens: epiqumew eimi gar nai o de endehs ara filos ekeinos e an endehs eimi dokew egw endehs de gignomai e an tis afairew pws de ou o oikew dh ws eoika o te eros kai o filios kai o epiqumia tugxanw eimi ws fainw eimi menecenos te kai lusis sumfhmi su ara ei filos eimi allhlwn fusis ph oikeios eimi su autos komidh fhmi kai ei ara tis eteros eteros epiqumew eimi de egw
english_head: that in which it is deficient, does it not? Yes. And the deficient is a friend to that in which it is deficient? I suppose so. And it becomes deficient in that of which it suffers a deprivation. To be sure. So it is one’s own belongings, it seems, that are the objects of love and friendship and desire; so it appears, Menexenus and Lysis. They both agreed. Then if you two are friends to each other by some natural bond you belong to one another. Precisely, they said. And in a case where

greek_head: ὦ παῖδες ἢ ἐρᾷ οὐκ ἄν ποτε ἐπεθύμει οὐδὲ ἤρα οὐδὲ ἐφίλει εἰ μὴ οἰκεῖός πῃ τῷ ἐρωμένῳ ἐτύγχανεν ὢν ἢ κατὰ τὴν
greek_tokens: eimi pais h eraomai ou an pote epiqumew oude eraw oude filew ei mh oikeios ph o eraomai tugxanw eimi h kata o yuxh h kata tis o yuxazw hqos h tropos h eidos panu ge fhmi o menecenos o de lusis sigaw eien eimi de egw o men dh fusis oikew anagkaion egw fainw filew eoika fhmi anagkaion ara o gnhsios erasths kai mh prospoihtos filew upo o paidikon
english_head: one person desires another, my boys, or loves him, he would never be desiring or loving or befriending him, unless he somehow belonged to his beloved either in soul, or in some disposition, demeanor or cast of soul. Yes, to be sure, said Menexenus; but Lysis was silent. Very well, said I: what belongs to us by nature has been shown to be something we needs must befriend. It seems so, he said. Then the genuine, not the pretended, lover must needs be befriended by his favorite.

greek_head: Ὁ μὲν οὖν Λύσις καὶ ὁ Μενέξενος μόγις πως ἐπενευσάτην ὁ δὲ Ἱπποθάλης ὑπὸ τῆς ἡδονῆς παντοδαπὰ ἠφίει χρώματα
greek_tokens: o men oun lusis kai o menecenos mogis pws epinew o de ippoqalhs upo o hdona pantodapos afihmi xrwma kai egw eipon boulomai o logos episkeptomai ei men tis o oikew o omoios diaferw legw an tis ws egw dokew eimi lusis te kai menecenos peri filos o eimi ei de tautos tugxanw eimi omoios te kai oikew ou radios apoballw o prosqen logos ws ou o omoios o omoios kata o omoioths axrhstos o de axrhstos filos
english_head: To this Lysis and Menexenus gave but a faint nod of assent; while Hippothales, in his delight, turned all manner of colors. So then, with the design of reviewing the argument, I proceeded: If there is any difference between what belongs and what is like, it seems to me, Lysis and Menexenus, that we might give some account of the meaning of friend. But if like and belonging are the same, it is not easy to get rid of our former statement, that the like is useless to the like in so far as they have likeness; and to admit that the useless is friendly

greek_head: ὁμολογεῖν πλημμελές βούλεσθ' οὖν ἦν δ' ἐγώ ἐπειδὴ ὥσπερ μεθύομεν ὑπὸ τοῦ λόγου συγχωρήσωμεν καὶ φῶμεν
greek_tokens: omologew plhmmelhs boulomai oun eimi de egw epei wsper mequw upo o logos sugxwrew kai fhmi eteros tis eimi o oikew o omoios panu ge poteros oun kai agaqos oikew tiqhmi pas o de kakos allotrios eimi h o men kakos o kakazw oikew o de agaqos o agaqos o de mhte agaqos mhte kakazw o mhte agaqos mhte kakos outws fhmi dokew sfeis ekastos ekastos
english_head: would be a gross mistake. So how if we agree now, I said, since our argument has made us quite tipsy, to say that the belonging and the like are two different things? By all means. Then shall we maintain that the good itself belongs to every one, while the bad is alien? Or does the bad belong to the bad, the good to the good, and what is neither good nor bad to what is neither good nor bad? They agreed that the last three pairs belong together. So here again, boys,

greek_head: οἰκεῖον εἶναι Πάλιν ἄρα ἦν δ' ἐγώ ὦ παῖδες οὓς τὸ πρῶτον λόγους ἀπεβαλόμεθα περὶ φιλίας εἰς τούτους
greek_tokens: oikew eimi palin ara eimi de egw eimi pais os o proteros logos apoballw peri filios eis outos eispiptw o gar adikos o adikos kai o kakos o kakazw oudeis hsswn filos eimi h o agaqos o agaqos eoika fhmi tis de o agaqos kai o oikew an tautos fhmi eimi allos tis h o agaqos o agaqos monos filos panu ge alla meis kai outos ge oiomai ecelegxw egw autos h ou mimnhskw mimnhskw
english_head: I said, we have dropped into the very statements regarding friendship which we rejected at first; for now the unjust will be as much a friend of the unjust, and the bad of the bad, as the good of the good. So it seems, he said. And what is more, if we say that the good and the belonging are the same, we cannot avoid making the good a friend only to the good. To be sure. But this again, you know, is a view of which we thought we had disabused ourselves; you remember, do you not? We do.

greek_head: Τί οὖν ἂν ἔτι χρησαίμεθα τῷ λόγῳ ἢ δῆλον ὅτι οὐδέν δέομαι οὖν ὥσπερ οἱ σοφοὶ ἐν τοῖς δικαστηρίοις τὰ εἰρημένα
greek_tokens: tis oun an eti xraw o logos h dhlos ostis oudeis deomai oun wsper e sofos en o dikasthrion o erw apas anapempazomai ei gar mhte e filew mhte e filew mhte e omoios mhte e anomoios mhte e agaqos mhte e oikeios mhte o allos osos dierxomai ou gar egw eti mimnhskw upo o plhqos alla ei mhdeis outos filos eimi egw men ouketi exw tis legos
english_head: So what more can we do with our argument? Obviously, I think, nothing. I can only ask you, accordingly, like the professional pleaders in the law courts, to perpend the whole of what has been said. If neither the loved nor the loving, nor the like nor the unlike, nor the good nor the belonging, nor all the rest that we have tried in turn—they are so many that I, for one, fail to remember any more—well, if none of these is a friend, I am at a loss for anything further to say.

greek_head: Ταῦτα δ' εἰπὼν ἐν νῷ εἶχον ἄλλον ἤδη τινὰ τῶν πρεσβυτέρων κινεῖν κᾆτα ὥσπερ δαίμονές τινες προσελθόντες οἱ
greek_tokens: outos de eipon en egw exw allos hdh tis o presbus kinew eita wsper daimwn tis proserxomai e paidagwgos o te o menecenos kai o o lusidos exw autos o adelfos parakalew kai keleuw autos oikade apeimi hdh gar eimi oye o men oun proteros kai egw kai e periisthmi autos apelaunw epei de oudeis frontizw egw alla upobarbarizw aganaktew te kai
english_head: Having thus spoken, I was minded to stir up somebody else among the older people there; when, like spirits from another world, there came upon us the tutors of Menexenus and Lysis: they were bringing along the boys’ brothers, and called out to them the order to go home; for it was getting late. At first we tried, with the help of the group around us, to drive the tutors off; but they took no notice of us at all, and went on angrily calling, as before, in their foreign accent. We decided that

greek_head: οὐδὲν ἧττον ἐκάλουν ἀλλ' ἐδόκουν ἡμῖν ὑποπεπωκότες ἐν τοῖς Ἑρμαίοις ἄποροι εἶναι προσφέρεσθαι ἡττηθέντες οὖν αὐτῶν
greek_tokens: oudeis hsswn kalew alla dokew egw upopinw en o ermaion aporos eimi prosferw hssaomai oun autos dialuw o sunousia omws de egw hdh apeimi autos nun men eimi de egw eimi lusis te kai menecenos katagelastos gignomai egw te gerwn anhr kai su erew gar ode apeimi ws oiomai egw allhlwn filos eimi kai egw gar en su tiqhmi oupw de ostis eimi o filos oios te gignomai eceuriskw
english_head: they had taken a drop too much at the festival and might be awkward customers; so we gave in to them, and broke up our party. However, just as they were moving off, I remarked: Today, Lysis and Menexenus, we have made ourselves ridiculous—I, an old man, as well as you. For these others will go away and tell how we believe we are friends of one another—for I count myself in with you—but what a friend is, we have not yet succeeded in discovering.
